> **CRITICAL — The Two s-Domain Differentiator Formulas (must NOT be confused):**
> - $L\{\frac{df}{dt}\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$
> - $L\{\frac{d^2f}{dt^2}\} = s^2 F(s) - s f(0^-) - f'(0^-)$

## 2.3 Standard Laplace Transform Pairs (Master Table)

| # | $f(t)$ for $t \geq 0$ | $F(s) = L\{f(t)\}$ | ROC |
|---|----------------------|---------------------|-----|
| 1 | $\delta(t)$ (Unit Impulse) | $1$ | All $s$ |
| 2 | $u(t)$ (Unit Step) | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 3 | $t$ (Ramp) | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 4 | $t^n$ (nth order ramp) | $\dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 5 | $e^{-at}$ (Decaying Exponential) | $\dfrac{1}{s + a}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 6 | $1 - e^{-at}$ | $\dfrac{a}{s(s + a)}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 7 | $\sin(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 8 | $\cos(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 9 | $e^{-at} \sin(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 10 | $e^{-at} \cos(\omega t)$ | $\dfrac{s + a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 11 | $t \cdot e^{-at}$ | $\dfrac{1}{(s+a)^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 12 | $\sinh(at)$ | $\dfrac{a}{s^2 - a^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ |
| 13 | $\cosh(at)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 - a^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Control Systems:** Transfer functions $H(s) = Y(s)/X(s)$ characterize systems.
- **Signal Processing:** Filter design (low-pass, high-pass) via pole-zero placement in $s$-plane.
- **Power Systems:** Transient stability analysis after faults.
- **Communication:** Laplace analysis of modulation/demodulation circuits.
- **Mechanical Systems:** Vibration analysis of beams, shock absorbers, and earthquake dampers.

## 2.5 Initial and Final Value Theorems — Conditions for Use

| Theorem | Formula | Condition to Apply |
|---------|---------|--------------------|
| **IVT** | $f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$ | $F(s)$ must be strictly proper (degree of numerator < degree of denominator) |
| **FVT** | $f(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$ | All poles of $s F(s)$ must lie strictly in the **left half plane** (LHP) |

> [!WARNING]
> **DO NOT** apply FVT if $F(s)$ has poles on the imaginary axis (e.g., $s = 0$ or $s = j\omega$). It will give you a meaningless result (often infinity). This is a common KTU board error.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Laplace Transform of $f(t) = e^{-at}$

We start from the formal definition and integrate directly.

$$L\{e^{-at}\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} \cdot e^{-st} \, dt$$

Combine the exponentials (since they have the same base $e$):

$$L\{e^{-at}\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t} \, dt$$

Integrate:

$$L\{e^{-at}\} = \left[ \frac{e^{-(s+a)t}}{-(s+a)} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

For convergence, we need $\text{Re}(s+a) > 0$, i.e., $\text{Re}(s) > -a$.

Applying the limits:

$$L\{e^{-at}\} = 0 - \left(\frac{e^{0}}{-(s+a)}\right) = \frac{1}{s+a}$$

**Final Result:** $\boxed{L\{e^{-at}\} = \frac{1}{s + a}, \quad \text{Re}(s) > -a}$

## 3.2 Derivation: Laplace Transform of $f(t) = \sin(\omega t)$

We use **Euler's formula** to convert sine into exponentials:

$$\sin(\omega t) = \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}$$

Apply linearity and use the result of Section 3.1:

$$L\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j} \left[ L\{e^{j\omega t}\} - L\{e^{-j\omega t}\} \right]$$

$$L\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j} \left[ \frac{1}{s - j\omega} - \frac{1}{s + j\omega} \right]$$

Combine over a common denominator:

$$L\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{(s + j\omega) - (s - j\omega)}{(s - j\omega)(s + j\omega)}$$

$$L\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{2j\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

$$L\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

**Final Result:** $\boxed{L\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}}$

## 3.3 Derivation: Differentiation Property

Let $g(t) = \frac{df(t)}{dt}$. Apply the definition:

$$L\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{df(t)}{dt} e^{-st} \, dt$$

Use integration by parts. Let $u = e^{-st}$ and $dv = \frac{df}{dt}dt$. Then $du = -s e^{-st} dt$ and $v = f(t)$.

$$\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du$$

Substituting:

$$L\left\{\frac{df}{dt}\right\} = \left[ f(t) e^{-st} \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) \cdot (-s e^{-st}) \, dt$$

$$L\left\{\frac{df}{dt}\right\} = \lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) e^{-st} - f(0) + s \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st} \, dt$$

For convergence, $f(t)$ is of exponential order, so $\lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) e^{-st} = 0$ for $\text{Re}(s) > \alpha$.

Therefore:

$$L\left\{\frac{df}{dt}\right\} = -f(0^-) + s F(s)$$

**Final Result:** $\boxed{L\left\{\frac{df(t)}{dt}\right\} = s F(s) - f(0^-)}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the use of $0^-$ (not $0^+$) for the initial value. This is the unilateral Laplace convention and is critical when there are impulses at $t = 0$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Inverse Laplace via Partial Fraction Expansion (Worked Example)

**Problem:** Find $L^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{s + 5}{s^2 + 3s + 2}\right\}$

**Step 1 — Factor the denominator:**

$$s^2 + 3s + 2 = (s+1)(s+2)$$

**Step 2 — Set up partial fractions:**

$$\frac{s + 5}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for constants using the cover-up method:**

For $A$, set $s = -1$:

$$A = \left.\frac{s+5}{s+2}\right|_{s=-1} = \frac{-1+5}{-1+2} = \frac{4}{1} = 4$$

For $B$, set $s = -2$:

$$B = \left.\frac{s+5}{s+1}\right|_{s=-2} = \frac{-2+5}{-2+1} = \frac{3}{-1} = -3$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite:**

$$\frac{s+5}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{4}{s+1} - \frac{3}{s+2}$$

**Step 5 — Apply inverse Laplace using the table:**

$$f(t) = 4 e^{-t} u(t) - 3 e^{-2t} u(t)$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{f(t) = (4 e^{-t} - 3 e^{-2t}) \, u(t)}$$

## 3.5 Derivation: Inverse Laplace with Complex Poles (Worked Example)

**Problem:** Find $L^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{s}{(s+2)^2 + 9}\right\}$

**Step 1 — Identify the form:** This matches the form $\frac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ with $a = 2$ and $\omega^2 = 9$, so $\omega = 3$.

**Step 2 — Rewrite the numerator to match the standard form:**

$$\frac{s}{(s+2)^2 + 9} = \frac{(s+2) - 2}{(s+2)^2 + 9} = \frac{s+2}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2} - \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{3}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply inverse Laplace using entries 9 and 10 from the master table:**

$$f(t) = e^{-2t} \cos(3t) \cdot u(t) - \frac{2}{3} e^{-2t} \sin(3t) \cdot u(t)$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{f(t) = e^{-2t}\left[\cos(3t) - \frac{2}{3}\sin(3t)\right] u(t)}$$

## 3.6 Derivation: Final Value Theorem Application

**Problem:** Find the steady-state value of $f(t)$ if $F(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(s+5)}$.

**Step 1 — Check pole locations:** Poles are at $s = 0$ and $s = -5$. The pole at $s = 0$ is on the imaginary axis, so FVT is **NOT directly applicable**. We must use a limiting approach.

**Step 2 — Use limit definition:**

$$f(\infty) = \lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s F(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{10}{s(s+5)} = \frac{10}{5} = 2$$

> [!NOTE]
> In this case, FVT works *despite* a pole at $s = 0$ because the pole is simple (first-order). The caution in Section 2.5 applies more strictly to repeated poles or poles on the imaginary axis at $s = j\omega \neq 0$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Laplace Transform Workflow in Network Analysis

The following diagram illustrates the **end-to-end pipeline** for solving a transient network problem using the Laplace Transform method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CIRCUIT PROBLEM IN TIME DOMAIN"]:::input --> B["STEP 1: Convert Elements to s-Domain Resistors Inductor becomes sL Capacitor becomes 1 over sC"]
    B --> C["STEP 2: Write KVL or KCL Algebraic Equation in s-Domain"]
    C --> D["STEP 3: Solve Algebraic Equation for Unknown Voltage or Current as F of s"]
    D --> E{"STEP 4: Check Form of F of s Rational Proper or Improper"}:::decision
    E -->|Proper| F["Apply Partial Fraction Expansion"]
    E -->|Improper| G["Perform Polynomial Long Division First"]
    G --> F
    F --> H["STEP 5: Use Laplace Pair Table to Convert Each Term to Time Domain"]
    H --> I["STEP 6: Combine Terms to Form Final Time Domain Solution v of t or i of t"]
    I --> J["STEP 7: Verify Using Initial and Final Value Theorems"]
    J --> K["FINAL TIME DOMAIN RESPONSE"]:::output

    classDef input fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 s-Domain Element Conversion Map (Block Architecture)

This diagram shows the **one-to-one mapping** from physical circuit elements to their s-domain equivalents, including initial-condition sources.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T_DOMAIN["TIME DOMAIN ELEMENTS"]
        R1["Resistor R ohms"]
        L1["Inductor L henries with initial current iL of 0"]
        C1["Capacitor C farads with initial voltage vC of 0"]
        V1["Voltage Source v t"]
        I1["Current Source i t"]
    end

    subgraph S_DOMAIN["S DOMAIN EQUIVALENTS"]
        R2["Impedance R"]
        L2["Impedance sL in series with Voltage Source L times iL of 0"]
        C2["Impedance 1 over sC in series with Voltage Source vC of 0 over s"]
        V2["Source V s"]
        I2["Source I s"]
    end

    R1 -->|"Laplaced"| R2
    L1 -->|"Laplaced"| L2
    C1 -->|"Laplaced"| C2
    V1 -->|"Laplaced"| V2
    I1 -->|"Laplaced"| I2

    classDef tdom fill:#FFEFD5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sdom fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class T_DOMAIN tdom
    class S_DOMAIN sdom
```

## 4.3 Partial Fraction Expansion Decision Tree

This decision tree shows how to choose the correct PFE form based on the nature of the poles.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["F of s = N of s over D of s"]:::start --> B{"Degree of N less than Degree of D"}:::dec
    B -->|Yes Proper| C["Proceed with PFE"]
    B -->|No Improper| D["Perform Long Division First Obtain Quotient and Remainder"]
    D --> C
    C --> E{"Nature of Poles of D of s"}:::dec2
    E -->|Real and Distinct| F1["Use A over s plus a plus B over s plus b plus ..."]
    E -->|Real and Repeated| F2["Use A1 over s plus a plus A2 over s plus a squared plus A3 over s plus a cubed plus ..."]
    E -->|Complex Conjugate| F3["Group as s plus a over s plus a squared plus omega squared plus omega over s plus a squared plus omega squared"]
    E -->|Mixed| F4["Apply combination of above cases"]

    F1 --> G["Apply Cover Up Method and Compare Coefficients"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H["Use Laplace Tables to Invert Each Term"]:::end

    classDef start fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
