Z-matrix Z1] S1B[Two-Port S2
Z-matrix Z2] S1A --- S1B SeriesResult[Overall Z = Z1 plus Z2] S1A --> SeriesResult S1B --> SeriesResult end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interconnection Insight:** The reason cascade uses T-matrices (multiplication) while parallel and series use Y and Z (addition) respectively is rooted in the definitions of those parameters — T-matrices express *output* in terms of *input*, making cascade composition simply a matrix product. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Define the following terms with respect to a two-port network: (i) Driving point impedance, (ii) Transfer impedance.** **Model Answer:** **(i) Driving Point Impedance:** It is the ratio of the port voltage to the port current at the **same port**, with the other port open- or short-circuited as required. Mathematically, $Z_{11} = \dfrac{V_1}{I_1}\bigg\vert_{I_2=0}$ and $Z_{22} = \dfrac{V_2}{I_2}\bigg\vert_{I_1=0}$. The term "driving point" refers to the fact that we are looking back into the port from which the network is being "driven." **(ii) Transfer Impedance:** It is the ratio of the voltage at one port to the current injected at the **other port**. $Z_{21} = \dfrac{V_2}{I_1}\bigg\vert_{I_2=0}$ and $Z_{12} = \dfrac{V_1}{I_2}\bigg\vert_{I_1=0}$. It characterizes how voltage is "transferred" from one port to the other. --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **List the six different two-port parameters. State the condition under which each parameter is measured.** **Model Answer:** | # | Parameter Set | Independent Variables | Measurement Condition | |---|---------------|----------------------|----------------------| | 1 | Z-parameters | $I_1, I_2$ | Port 2 open-circuited ($I_2 = 0$) | | 2 | Y-parameters | $V_1, V_2$ | Port 2 short-circuited ($V_2 = 0$) | | 3 | h-parameters | $I_1, V_2$ | $V_2 = 0$ for $h_{11}, h_{21}$; $I_1 = 0$ for $h_{12}, h_{22}$ | | 4 | g-parameters | $V_1, I_2$ | $I_2 = 0$ for $g_{11}, g_{21}$; $V_1 = 0$ for $g_{12}, g_{22}$ | | 5 | T (ABCD) | $V_2, I_2$ | Port 2 open for $A, C$; Port 2 short for $B, D$ | | 6 | T' (A'B'C'D') | $V_1, I_1$ | Port 1 open for $A', C'$; Port 1 short for $B', D'$ | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **(a)** Derive the expressions for converting **Z-parameters to Y-parameters** in a two-port network. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the **T-network** shown, with $Z_a = 5\,\Omega$, $Z_b = 3\,\Omega$, $Z_c = 2\,\Omega$, find the ABCD parameters. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** Starting from the Z-equation matrix form: $$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ Pre-multiply both sides by $\mathbf{Z}^{-1}$: $$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{Z}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ By definition, $\mathbf{Z}^{-1} = \mathbf{Y}$. The inverse of a $2\times 2$ matrix is: $$\mathbf{Z}^{-1} = \frac{1}{Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}} \begin{bmatrix} Z_{22} & -Z_{12} \\ -Z_{21} & Z_{11} \end{bmatrix}$$ Let $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}$. Therefore: $$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\Delta Z} \begin{bmatrix} Z_{22} & -Z_{12} \\ -Z_{21} & Z_{11} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$ Comparing with the Y-equation $I_1 = Y_{11} V_1 + Y_{12} V_2$, $I_2 = Y_{21} V_1 + Y_{22} V_2$: $$Y_{11} = \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{12} = \frac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{21} = \frac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{22} = \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z}$$ This is the desired conversion. (7 marks: 3 for setup, 2 for matrix inversion, 2 for final expressions) ✓ **Model Solution for (b):** **Step 1:** Compute Z-parameters of the T-network using the formula derived in Section 3.4. $$Z_{11} = Z_a + Z_c = 5 + 2 = 7\,\Omega$$ $$Z_{22} = Z_b + Z_c = 3 + 2 = 5\,\Omega$$ $$Z_{12} = Z_{21} = Z_c = 2\,\Omega$$ **Step 2:** Compute $\Delta Z$. $$\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21} = (7)(5) - (2)(2) = 35 - 4 = 31$$ **Step 3:** Apply the Z → ABCD conversion formulas (derived in Section 3.2). $$A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{7}{2} = 3.5$$ $$B = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}} = \frac{31}{2} = 15.5\,\Omega$$ $$C = \frac{1}{Z_{21}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5\,\text{S}$$ $$D = \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5$$ **Verification:** For a reciprocal T-network, $AD - BC$ should equal 1. $$AD - BC = (3.5)(2.5) - (15.5)(0.5) = 8.75 - 7.75 = 1 \checkmark$$ $$\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.5 & 15.5\,\Omega \\ 0.5\,\text{S} & 2.5 \end{bmatrix}}$$ (7 marks: 2 for Z-params, 1 for $\Delta Z$, 3 for ABCD values, 1 for verification) --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **(a)** Two two-port networks with **T-matrices** $T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ are cascaded. Find the overall ABCD parameters. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the relation between **Z-parameters and h-parameters** of a two-port network. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1:** Recall the cascade rule. For two two-port networks connected in cascade, the overall T-matrix is the **product** of the individual T-matrices. $$T = T_1 \times T_2$$ **Step 2:** Multiply the matrices. $$T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ Element-wise: $$A = (1)(1) + (2)(1) = 1 + 2 = 3$$ $$B = (1)(1) + (2)(1) = 1 + 2 = 3$$ $$C = (0.5)(1) + (1)(1) = 0.5 + 1 = 1.5$$ $$D = (0.5)(1) + (1)(1) = 0.5 + 1 = 1.5$$ $$\boxed{T_{\text{overall}} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 3 \\ 1.5 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix}}$$ **Step 3:** Verify reciprocity: $AD - BC = (3)(1.5) - (3)(1.5) = 0$. Wait — this is **NOT** equal to 1! > [!WARNING] > **Critical Observation:** The result $AD - BC = 0$ indicates that the **second network is not reciprocal**. The matrix $T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ does not satisfy the reciprocity condition $AD - BC = 1$. In KTU exams, this should be flagged as a conceptual note. (7 marks: 2 for cascade principle, 4 for multiplication, 1 for verification) **Model Solution for (b):** **Starting point:** Z-equations and h-equations. $$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2 \quad \text{...(Z1)}$$ $$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2 \quad \text{...(Z2)}$$ $$V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2 \quad \text{...(h1)}$$ $$I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2 \quad \text{...(h2)}$$ **Step 1:** Rearrange (Z2) to express $I_2$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_1$. $$I_2 = \frac{V_2 - Z_{21} I_1}{Z_{22}} = -\frac{Z_{21}}{Z_{22}} I_1 + \frac{1}{Z_{22}} V_2$$ **Step 2:** Compare with (h2): $I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2$. $$h_{21} = -\frac{Z_{21}}{Z_{22}}, \quad h_{22} = \frac{1}{Z_{22}}$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $I_2$ from Step 1 back into (Z1). $$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} \left( -\frac{Z_{21}}{Z_{22}} I_1 + \frac{1}{Z_{22}} V_2 \right)$$ $$V_1 = \left( Z_{11} - \frac{Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_{22}} \right) I_1 + \frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{22}} V_2$$ $$V_1 = \frac{Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_{22}} I_1 + \frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{22}} V_2$$ **Step 4:** Compare with (h1): $V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2$. $$h_{11} = \frac{Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_{22}} = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{22}}, \quad h_{12} = \frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{22}}$$ **Final Z → h Conversion Formulas:** $$\boxed{h_{11} = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{22}}, \quad h_{12} = \frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{22}}, \quad h_{21} = \frac{-Z_{21}}{Z_{22}}, \quad h_{22} = \frac{1}{Z_{22}}}$$ (7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for $h_{21}, h_{22}$, 3 for $h_{11}, h_{12}$) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign convention error in ABCD:** Forgetting the negative sign on $I_2$ in $\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = T \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix}$ is the **#1 mistake**. This changes the entire sign of $B$ and $D$ conversion formulas. > 2. **Confusing $Z_{21}$ and $Z_{12}$:** They are equal *only* for reciprocal networks. For non-reciprocal networks (e.g., with controlled sources), they are different. > 3. **Forgetting to check $AD - BC = 1$:** Always verify reciprocity for sanity-check. If your answer violates it, you've likely made a sign error. > 4. **Using the wrong parameter for interconnection:** Cascade → multiply $T$. Series → add $Z$. Parallel → add $Y$. Mixing these up costs full marks. > 5. **Not including units in the answer:** $A$ and $D$ are dimensionless. $B$ has units of $\Omega$. $C$ has units of siemens (S). Examiners *do* deduct marks for sloppy units. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Network Functions & Two-Port Networks:** - **Network Function:** $H(s) = R(s)/E(s)$ with zero initial conditions. Always a rational function of $s$. - **Two main categories:** Driving point (same port) vs. Transfer (different port). - **Six parameter sets:** Z, Y, h, g, T (ABCD), T' (A'B'C'D'). All are equivalent descriptions. - **Z-parameters** require $I_2 = 0$ (Port 2 open). Use them when the port is naturally open. - **Y-parameters** require $V_2 = 0$ (Port 2 short). Use them when the port is naturally shorted. - **h-parameters** are "hybrid" — series input, shunt output. **Standard for transistors.** - **ABCD parameters** express output in terms of input with $I_2$ negated. **Best for cascade.** - **Reciprocity conditions:** - $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ - $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ - $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ - $A D - B C = 1$ - **Symmetry conditions (stronger than reciprocity):** - $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ - $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ - $h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = 1$ - $A = D$ - **T-Network Z-matrix (MUST MEMORIZE):** $$\mathbf{Z}_T = \begin{bmatrix} Z_a + Z_c & Z_c \\ Z_c & Z_b + Z_c \end{bmatrix}$$ - **π-Network Y-matrix (MUST MEMORIZE):** $$\mathbf{Y}_\pi = \begin{bmatrix} Y_a + Y_c & -Y_c \\ -Y_c & Y_b + Y_c \end{bmatrix}$$ where $Y_a, Y_b, Y_c$ are the admittances of the π arms. - **Interconnection rules (memorize the parameter and the operation):** - Cascade → $T = T_1 T_2$ (multiply) - Series → $Z = Z_1 + Z_2$ (add) - Parallel → $Y = Y_1 + Y_2$ (add) - Series-Parallel → use $h$ - Parallel-Series → use $g$ - **Conversion shortcut:** To go Z → ABCD, divide each Z-parameter by $Z_{21}$. To go Y → ABCD, divide each Y-parameter by $-Y_{21}$ and flip signs appropriately. - **Units sanity check:** $A, D$ → dimensionless; $B$ → $\Omega$; $C$ → S; $Z$ → $\Omega$; $Y$ → S; $h_{11}$ → $\Omega$; $h_{22}$ → S; $h_{12}, h_{21}$ → dimensionless. - **Image impedance** is the impedance at which the network is "matched" — useful in classical filter design. - **Characterization impedance** is the geometric mean of $Z_{oc}$ and $Z_{sc}$ at a port: $Z_0 = \sqrt{Z_{oc} Z_{sc}}$.

topics.title: Properties of Driving Point and Transfer Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT414 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Properties of Driving Point and Transfer Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Driving Point Function (DPF)

A **Driving Point Function (DPF)** is a network function in which the input (excitation) and output (response) variables are measured at the *same* port (same pair of terminals) of a one-port or two-port network.

### Formal Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Driving Point Impedance Function** ($Z(s)$ or $Z_{11}(s)$):
> The ratio of the Laplace transform of the voltage across a port to the Laplace transform of the current entering the same port, with *all initial conditions set to zero* and *no independent sources inside the network*.

$$Z(s) = \frac{V(s)}{I(s)} \bigg|_{\text{zero IC, no internal sources}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Driving Point Admittance Function** ($Y(s)$ or $Y_{11}(s)$):
> The reciprocal of the driving point impedance — the ratio of current to voltage at the same port.

$$Y(s) = \frac{I(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{1}{Z(s)}$$

The driving point is called the **port** at which the network is "driven" by the source. The function is, hence, literally the impedance/admittance *seen looking into* that port.

---

## 1.2 Transfer Function (TF)

A **Transfer Function** is a network function in which the input (excitation) and output (response) variables are measured at *different* ports or at *different* points inside the network.

### Four Canonical Transfer Functions

| Symbol | Name | Definition |
|--------|------|------------|
| $G(s)$ or $G_{12}(s)$ | Voltage Transfer Ratio | $V_2(s)/V_1(s)$ |
| $\alpha(s)$ | Current Transfer Ratio | $I_2(s)/I_1(s)$ |
| $Z_{12}(s)$ | Transfer Impedance | $V_2(s)/I_1(s)$ |
| $Y_{12}(s)$ | Transfer Admittance | $I_2(s)/V_1(s)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For a two-port network, the four **two-port parameters** ($z$, $y$, $h$, $g$) are all transfer functions except $z_{11}, z_{22}, y_{11}, y_{22}$ which are driving point functions. This is exactly why the study of driving-point and transfer functions is foundational to **two-port network analysis**.

---

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **garden hose connected to a tap (faucet)**:

- The **tap** is the input port (where voltage is applied = water pressure).
- The **sprinkler end** is the output port (where water flows out = load).
- The **DPF** is the resistance the hose offers when you push water through it — measured *at the tap itself*.
- The **Transfer Function** is how much water pressure reaches the sprinkler end given the pressure at the tap — measured *between two points*.

A DPF tells you *"what does the network look like from where I'm standing?"*. A TF tells you *"how does the network pass my signal onward?"*.

---

## 1.4 Why the Properties Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU asks students to memorize **7 standard properties** of network functions and apply them to (i) test whether a given expression is a *valid* network function, and (ii) synthesize a network from a given rational function. Both questions appear repeatedly in the KTU university exam.

The properties ensure the **causality**, **stability**, and **realizability** of any physical network.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standard Form of a Network Function

Every driving-point or transfer function of a lumped, linear, time-invariant (LTI) network with *passive* elements takes the **rational form**:

$$N(s) = \frac{P(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{a_m s^m + a_{m-1} s^{m-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0}{b_n s^n + b_{n-1} s^{n-1} + \cdots + b_1 s + b_0}$$

where $m \le n$ and all coefficients $a_i, b_j$ are **real numbers** (because the network is composed of real-valued R, L, C, M components).

It can be factored into **pole-zero form**:

$$N(s) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{m}(s - z_i)}{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(s - p_j)}$$

where $z_i$ are **zeros** and $p_j$ are **poles** of the function.

---

## 2.2 The Seven KTU-Mandated Properties

### Property 1: Real Coefficients

The coefficients $a_0, a_1, \ldots, a_m, b_0, b_1, \ldots, b_n$ of $P(s)$ and $Q(s)$ must be **real numbers** (no complex coefficients).

> **Why?** Because the network is built from elements with real-valued parameters (R, L, C, M). The differential equations describing them have real coefficients, and so do their Laplace transforms.

---

### Property 2: Complex Poles/Zeros Occur in Conjugate Pairs

If $p_k = \sigma_k + j\omega_k$ is a pole (or zero), then $p_k^* = \sigma_k - j\omega_k$ must also be a pole (or zero).

> **Why?** Real-coefficient polynomials can only have roots that are either real *or* come in conjugate pairs.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget this and write transfer functions with a *single* complex pole like $(s - 1 + j2)$, which violates reality of coefficients.

---

### Property 3: Poles on the Imaginary Axis Must Be Simple

If $p_j = j\omega_j$ is a pole on the $j\omega$-axis, then it must be a **simple (first-order) pole**. Repeated imaginary-axis poles are forbidden.

> **Why?** A repeated pole on the $j\omega$-axis produces a term like $t\sin(\omega t)$ in the time-domain response, which grows without bound — making the network unstable.

---

### Property 4: No Poles in the Right-Half s-Plane (RHP)

All poles must lie in the **left-half s-plane** (LHP): $\text{Re}(p_j) \le 0$.

> **Why?** Each pole contributes a term $K e^{p_j t}$ to the impulse response. For stability (bounded output for bounded input), we need $\text{Re}(p_j) \le 0$. For an LC or RLC passive network, poles lie strictly on the $j\omega$-axis or in the LHP.

> **Exception (for L-C networks):** Purely reactive LC networks can have simple poles on the $j\omega$-axis (these are lossless resonances).

---

### Property 5: No Missing Powers of s in $P(s)$ and $Q(s)$

For a **driving point function of an LC (lossless) network**, the numerator and denominator polynomials must contain **only even powers of $s$** or **only odd powers of $s$**.

For a **driving point impedance of an RC network**, the numerator has the same or higher power of $s$ than the denominator term-by-term (a special structure called *Foster's theorem*).

> **Why?** The Foster Reactance Theorem states that the impedance of an LC network is a *reactance function*, which has this alternating structure.

---

### Property 6: The Degree of $P(s)$ ≤ Degree of $Q(s)$

For a **driving point admittance**, $m \le n$ (numerator degree ≤ denominator degree).

For a **driving point impedance** of an R-L network, the opposite holds: $m \ge n$.

> **Why?** At $s \to \infty$, an R-L network behaves as an open circuit (infinite impedance, $Z \to \infty$), so $Z(s) \to \infty$, requiring $m > n$.

---

### Property 7: Low-Frequency and High-Frequency Behavior

- $Z_{DP}(s \to 0)$ and $Y_{DP}(s \to 0)$ are **finite, positive, real constants** for passive networks.
- The behavior as $s \to \infty$ is governed by the highest-power term — must be a *real, non-negative* value.
- For **RL/RC/RLC networks**, $Z(s)$ is a **positive-real (PR) function**.

> **Why?** Positive-realness guarantees that the real part of impedance is non-negative for all $\text{Re}(s) > 0$, which is the *passivity* condition.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Mathematical Statement | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Real coefficients | $a_i, b_j \in \mathbb{R}$ | Network built from real elements |
| 2 | Conjugate pairs | If $p_k \in \mathbb{C}$, then $p_k^* \in \text{roots}$ | Real-coefficient polynomials |
| 3 | Simple imaginary-axis poles | $\text{multiplicity}(j\omega_0) = 1$ | Stability of lossless network |
| 4 | LHP poles | $\text{Re}(p_j) < 0$ (or $=0$ simple) | BIBO stability |
| 5a | LC impedance structure | $Z_{LC}(s) = \text{odd}/{\text{even}}$ or $\text{even}/\text{odd}$ | Foster Reactance Theorem |
| 5b | RC/RL structure | Term-by-term degree comparison | Foster's Theorem on RC |
| 6 | DPF admittance degree | $m \le n$ | At $\infty$, $Y \to 0$ (open) |
| 7 | Positive-real | $\text{Re}\,Z(j\omega) \ge 0$ | Passivity |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These properties form the **theoretical bedrock of analog filter design**. When you design a Butterworth or Chebyshev filter in `MATLAB`, the underlying transfer function is built so that all 7 properties are satisfied. **Stability analysis** of control systems (root locus, Nyquist plots) is also based on these properties.

In the **biomedical field**, EEG/ECG amplifier transfer functions must satisfy Property 7 (positive-realness) so they don't inject energy into the patient.

In **power systems**, the driving-point impedance at the point-of-common-coupling (PCC) determines harmonic resonance behavior — again, all governed by these seven properties.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Coefficients Must Be Real

Consider a simple series R-L circuit driven by a voltage source $V(s)$.

### Step 1: Write the KVL equation

$$V(s) = R\,I(s) + L\,s\,I(s)$$

### Step 2: Solve for the driving-point impedance

$$\begin{aligned}
V(s) &= I(s)\,(R + Ls) \\[4pt]
Z(s) &= \frac{V(s)}{I(s)} = R + Ls
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Identify the polynomial structure

$$\begin{aligned}
P(s) &= Ls + R \\
Q(s) &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Comment on coefficients

$L, R \in \mathbb{R}$ because they represent real physical quantities (inductance in henries, resistance in ohms). **Therefore all coefficients are real**, establishing Property 1.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Complex Poles Must Come in Conjugate Pairs

Suppose a network function has a single complex pole at $s = -1 + j2$:

$$N(s) = \frac{1}{(s - (-1 + j2))} = \frac{1}{s + 1 - j2}$$

### Step 1: Expand using partial fractions or find the impulse response

$$h(t) = e^{(-1+j2)t}\,u(t)$$

### Step 2: Apply Euler's identity

$$\begin{aligned}
h(t) &= e^{-t}\,e^{j2t}\,u(t) \\[4pt]
&= e^{-t}\bigl(\cos 2t + j\sin 2t\bigr)\,u(t)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Identify the problem

The impulse response is **complex-valued**, but a real network's impulse response must be **real**. This is a contradiction.

### Step 4: Resolve by adding the conjugate pole

We must also include the conjugate pole $s = -1 - j2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
N(s) &= \frac{K}{(s + 1 - j2)(s + 1 + j2)} = \frac{K}{(s+1)^2 + 4}
\end{aligned}$$

The coefficients are now real. The impulse response becomes:

$$h(t) = 2K\,e^{-t}\cos(2t)\,u(t) \quad \in \mathbb{R}$$

This proves **Property 2**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Why Poles Must Not Lie in the RHP (Stability)

Consider the impulse response contribution from a single pole $p_j$:

$$h_j(t) = K_j\,e^{p_j t}\,u(t)$$

### Step 1: Substitute $p_j = \sigma_j + j\omega_j$

$$h_j(t) = K_j\,e^{\sigma_j t}\,e^{j\omega_j t}\,u(t)$$

### Step 2: Examine the magnitude

$$\vert h_j(t) \vert = \vert K_j \vert\,e^{\sigma_j t}$$

### Step 3: Apply the BIBO stability condition

For **bounded-input, bounded-output (BIBO)** stability, we need the impulse response to be absolutely integrable:

$$\int_0^\infty \vert h_j(t) \vert\, dt < \infty$$

$$\int_0^\infty \vert K_j \vert\,e^{\sigma_j t}\, dt < \infty$$

### Step 4: Solve the integral

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_0^\infty e^{\sigma_j t}\,dt &= \left[\frac{e^{\sigma_j t}}{\sigma_j}\right]_0^\infty
\end{aligned}$$

This integral converges **only if** $\sigma_j < 0$, i.e., $\text{Re}(p_j) < 0$.

### Step 5: Conclusion

Therefore, all poles of a stable network function must lie strictly in the **left-half s-plane**. Poles on the imaginary axis are allowed only as **simple** poles (marginally stable), proving **Properties 3 and 4**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: The Foster Reactance Theorem (Property 5a for LC)

Consider a lossless LC one-port. Its impedance must be a **purely imaginary** quantity at $s = j\omega$:

$$Z(j\omega) = jX(\omega)$$

where $X(\omega)$ is the **reactance**.

### Step 1: Build a partial-fraction expansion

Any reactance function can be decomposed as:

$$Z(s) = K_\infty s + \frac{K_0}{s} + \sum_{k} \frac{2K_k s}{s^2 + \omega_k^2}$$

where every $K_i \ge 0$.

### Step 2: Find the common denominator

The structure of the partial fractions is:
- Each term is a **ratio of odd to even** powers of $s$ (since $\frac{K_0}{s}$, $K_\infty s$, $\frac{2K_k s}{s^2 + \omega_k^2}$ all have this form).

### Step 3: Conclude the structure

When you sum fractions with denominators $(s^2 + \omega_k^2)$, the common denominator always becomes an **even polynomial** in $s$, and the numerator becomes an **odd polynomial** in $s$.

$$\boxed{Z_{LC}(s) = \frac{\text{odd polynomial in } s}{\text{even polynomial in } s} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\text{even polynomial in } s}{\text{odd polynomial in } s}}$$

This proves **Property 5a**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Testing Whether a Function is a Valid DPF

**Question:** Is the following a valid driving-point impedance?

$$Z(s) = \frac{s^2 + 4s + 3}{s^2 + 2s + 5}$$

**Solution:**

### Step 1: Check coefficients
All coefficients are real. ✅ **Property 1** satisfied.

### Step 2: Find the poles
$Q(s) = s^2 + 2s + 5 = 0$

$$\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{4 - 20}}{2} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{-2 \pm \sqrt{-16}}{2} = -1 \pm j2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3: Apply Property 2 (conjugate pairs)
Poles are at $-1 + j2$ and $-1 - j2$ — **conjugate pair**. ✅ **Property 2** satisfied.

### Step 4: Apply Property 4 (LHP location)
$\text{Re}(p) = -1 < 0$. ✅ **Property 4** satisfied.

### Step 5: Check Property 6 (degree condition)
$m = 2$, $n = 2$. For a general impedance $m \le n+1$, so $m = n = 2$ is acceptable. ✅

### Step 6: Check positive-realness (Property 7)

$$\begin{aligned}
Z(j\omega) &= \frac{(j\omega)^2 + 4(j\omega) + 3}{(j\omega)^2 + 2(j\omega) + 5} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{-\omega^2 + 3 + 4j\omega}{-\omega^2 + 5 + 2j\omega}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator:

$$\text{Re}\,Z(j\omega) = \frac{(-\omega^2+3)(-\omega^2+5) + 8\omega^2}{(-\omega^2+5)^2 + 4\omega^2}$$

The denominator is always positive. The numerator is:

$$\omega^4 - 8\omega^2 + 15 + 8\omega^2 = \omega^4 + 15 > 0$$

So $\text{Re}\,Z(j\omega) > 0$ for all $\omega$. ✅ **Property 7** satisfied.

### Conclusion
$Z(s)$ is a **valid driving-point impedance** of a passive network. ✅

---

## 3.6 Counter-Example: Identifying Invalid Network Functions

**Test the candidate:** $H(s) = \frac{s - 3}{s^2 + s + 1}$

### Check 1: Pole location
Poles of $Q(s) = s^2 + s + 1$:

$$s = \frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 4}}{2} = -0.5 \pm j\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

Poles are in LHP. ✅

### Check 2: Zero location
Zero at $s = 3$ — in **RHP**. ⚠️

For a transfer function, RHP zeros are *allowed* (they cause non-minimum phase behavior). However, for a **driving-point impedance**, this violates positive-realness and is **not realizable** as a passive network DPF.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Port Network Block Diagram Showing DPF and TF

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXCITE[Excitation Source]
        VS[("V1 s = Voltage source")]
        IS[("I1 s = Current source")]
    end

    subgraph NET[Linear Passive Two-Port Network N]
        P1([Port 1: Driving Point])
        P2([Port 2: Output Port])
        NET_CORE{{Impedance / Admittance Matrix Z11 Z12 Z21 Z22}}
        P1 --> NET_CORE
        NET_CORE --> P2
    end

    subgraph OUT[Response]
        VR[("V2 s")]
        IR[("I2 s")]
    end

    VS --> P1
    IS --> P1
    P2 --> VR
    P2 --> IR

    DPF_Z["Z11 s = V1 / I1 — DRIVING POINT IMPEDANCE"]
    DPF_Y["Y11 s = I1 / V1 — DRIVING POINT ADMITTANCE"]
    TF_V["G12 s = V2 / V1 — VOLTAGE TRANSFER"]
    TF_I["A21 s = I2 / I1 — CURRENT TRANSFER"]
    TF_Z["Z21 s = V2 / I1 — TRANSFER IMPEDANCE"]
    TF_Y["Y21 s = I2 / V1 — TRANSFER ADMITTANCE"]

    P1 -.measured at.-> DPF_Z
    P1 -.measured at.-> DPF_Y
    P1 -.input.-> TF_V
    P1 -.input.-> TF_I
    P1 -.input.-> TF_Z
    P1 -.input.-> TF_Y
    VR -.output.-> TF_V
    IR -.output.-> TF_I
    VR -.output.-> TF_Z
    IR -.output.-> TF_Y
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Driving-point functions have their input *and* output at the *same* port (Port 1). Transfer functions have their input at Port 1 and output at Port 2.

---

## 4.2 Pole-Zero Plot Pattern for a Valid Network Function

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPlane["Complex s-Plane — Allowed Pole Locations"]
        AX["Real Axis sigma"]
        IMA["Imaginary Axis j omega"]

        subgraph LHP["LEFT HALF PLANE Allowed: All poles here"]
            P1["p = -2 + j3 — simple pole"]
            P2["p = -2 - j3 — conjugate pole"]
            P3["p = -5 — real pole"]
        end

        subgraph IAX["IMAGINARY AXIS Allowed ONLY if simple"]
            P4["p = + j4 — simple pole"]
            P5["p = - j4 — conjugate pole"]
        end

        subgraph RHP["RIGHT HALF PLANE FORBIDDEN for poles"]
            FORB["No poles allowed here"]
        end
    end

    LHP -.forbidden.-> RHP
    IAX -.must be simple multiplicity 1.-> IAX
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow of the Seven-Property Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Candidate Network Function Ns = P s / Q s"]
    A --> B{"Property 1: Are all coefficients real?"}
    B -- No --> X1["REJECT"]
    B -- Yes --> C{"Property 2: Complex poles zeros in conjugate pairs?"}
    C -- No --> X1
    C -- Yes --> D{"Property 3: Imaginary axis poles simple?"}
    D -- No --> X1
    D -- Yes --> E{"Property 4: All poles in LHP?"}
    E -- No --> X1
    E -- Yes --> F{"Property 5: For LC RC — correct power structure?"}
    F -- No --> X1
    F -- Yes --> G{"Property 6: Degree conditions satisfied?"}
    G -- No --> X1
    G -- Yes --> H{"Property 7: Positive real if DPF?"}
    H -- No --> X1
    H -- Yes --> Y["ACCEPT: Valid network function"]

    classDef reject fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef accept fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    class X1 reject
    class Y accept
    class B,C,D,E,F,G,H decision
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define driving point impedance and driving point admittance of a one-port network. State the condition for which a driving point function represents a passive network.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **driving point impedance** $Z(s)$ is the ratio of the Laplace-transformed voltage across the port to the Laplace-transformed current entering the same port, with all initial conditions zero:

$$Z(s) = \frac{V(s)}{I(s)}\bigg|_{\text{zero IC}}$$

The **driving point admittance** is its reciprocal:

$$Y(s) = \frac{I(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{1}{Z(s)}$$

The driving point function represents a **passive network** if and only if it is a **positive-real (PR) function**, i.e., (i) $Z(s)$ is real when $s$ is real, (ii) $\text{Re}\,Z(s) \ge 0$ for $\text{Re}(s) > 0$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four properties that a transfer function of a network must satisfy.**

**Model Answer (1 mark per property, total 4 → take any 3 for 3 marks):**

1. All coefficients of the numerator and denominator polynomials must be **real numbers**.
2. Poles and zeros, if complex, must occur in **conjugate pairs**.
3. The real parts of all poles must be **less than or equal to zero** (LHP or simple imaginary axis poles) for stability.
4. The degree of the numerator polynomial cannot exceed the degree of the denominator polynomial by more than 1 for a driving-point impedance.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** For the network shown in the figure (a series RLC circuit with $R = 2\,\Omega$, $L = 1\,H$, $C = 0.5\,F$), find the **driving point impedance** $Z(s)$. List all properties of $Z(s)$ and verify each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State and prove the property that *"the poles and zeros of a network function, if complex, must occur in conjugate pairs"*. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a)

**Step 1: Draw the network and write the KVL equation**

For a series R-L-C circuit:

$$V(s) = R\,I(s) + L\,s\,I(s) + \frac{1}{Cs}\,I(s)$$

**Step 2: Substitute values** $R = 2$, $L = 1$, $C = 0.5$

$$\begin{aligned}
V(s) &= 2\,I(s) + s\,I(s) + \frac{1}{0.5\,s}\,I(s) \\[4pt]
&= \left(2 + s + \frac{2}{s}\right) I(s)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Find the DPF**

$$Z(s) = \frac{V(s)}{I(s)} = 2 + s + \frac{2}{s} = \frac{s^2 + 2s + 2}{s}$$

**Step 4: Verify Property 1 (Real Coefficients)**

Numerator: $1, 2, 2$ — all real. ✅
Denominator: $1, 0$ — all real. ✅

**Step 5: Find poles and zeros**

Zeros: $s^2 + 2s + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow s = -1 \pm j1$
Poles: $s = 0$ (simple pole on the imaginary axis)

**Step 6: Verify Property 2 (Conjugate pairs)**

Zeros $-1+j1$ and $-1-j1$ are conjugate. ✅

**Step 7: Verify Property 3 (Simple imaginary-axis pole)**

The pole at $s = 0$ has multiplicity 1. ✅

**Step 8: Verify Property 4 (Poles in LHP)**

Pole at $s = 0$ is on the imaginary axis, not in the RHP. ✅ (allowed because it is simple).

**Step 9: Verify Property 7 (Positive-realness)**

Compute $\text{Re}\,Z(j\omega)$:

$$Z(j\omega) = \frac{-\omega^2 + 2 + 2j\omega}{j\omega} = \frac{-\omega^2 + 2}{j\omega} + 2$$

$$Z(j\omega) = -j\frac{\omega^2 - 2}{\omega} + 2$$

$$\text{Re}\,Z(j\omega) = 2 > 0 \quad \forall \omega$$

✅ Positive-real.

**Conclusion:** $Z(s) = \dfrac{s^2 + 2s + 2}{s}$ is a valid DPF of a passive network. **(7 marks awarded for correct expression + verification of all properties)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b)

**Statement:** *"If a network function $N(s)$ has real coefficients, then any complex pole or zero must occur as part of a complex-conjugate pair."*

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Suppose $p = \sigma + j\omega$ is a pole of $N(s)$, where $\omega \ne 0$. Then $(s - p)$ divides the denominator polynomial $Q(s)$.

**Step 2:** Since the coefficients of $Q(s)$ are real, the **complex conjugate** $p^* = \sigma - j\omega$ must also satisfy $Q(p^*) = 0$ (because polynomial evaluation commutes with conjugation: $\overline{Q(s)} = Q(\overline{s})$).

**Step 3:** Therefore $p^*$ is also a root, i.e., also a pole of $N(s)$.

**Step 4:** Hence the poles come in conjugate pairs. The same argument applies to zeros.

**Step 5 (Physical interpretation):** A real physical network produces a real-valued impulse response $h(t) \in \mathbb{R}$. If only a single complex pole $p = \sigma + j\omega$ existed, its contribution would be:

$$h_p(t) = K\,e^{\sigma t}\bigl(\cos\omega t + j\sin\omega t\bigr)\,u(t)$$

which is complex-valued, violating the reality of $h(t)$. Adding the conjugate pole cancels the imaginary part and gives:

$$h_{p,p^*}(t) = 2K\,e^{\sigma t}\cos\omega t\,u(t) \in \mathbb{R}$$

**Q.E.D. (7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Test whether the following function is a valid driving-point admittance of a passive network:

$$Y(s) = \frac{s^2 + 4s + 3}{s^2 + 2s + 5}$$

If not valid, explain why. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the conditions under which a rational function with real coefficients represents a stable network. State the **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** and use it to check stability of $Q(s) = s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 2$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B(a)

**Step 1: Identify the structure**

$$Y(s) = \frac{s^2 + 4s + 3}{s^2 + 2s + 5} = \frac{(s+1)(s+3)}{(s+1)^2 + 4}$$

**Step 2: Find the poles**

$$s^2 + 2s + 5 = 0 \Rightarrow s = -1 \pm j2$$

Both poles are in the LHP. ✅ **Property 4**

**Step 3: Conjugate pair check**
$-1+j2$ and $-1-j2$ are conjugates. ✅ **Property 2**

**Step 4: Real coefficients**
All coefficients are real. ✅ **Property 1**

**Step 5: Degree condition**

For DPF admittance, $m \le n$: here $m = n = 2$. ✅ **Property 6**

**Step 6: Positive-realness test**

Compute $Y(j\omega)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Y(j\omega) &= \frac{(j\omega)^2 + 4(j\omega) + 3}{(j\omega)^2 + 2(j\omega) + 5} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3 - \omega^2 + 4j\omega}{5 - \omega^2 + 2j\omega}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(5 - \omega^2) - 2j\omega$:

$$\text{Re}\,Y(j\omega) = \frac{(3-\omega^2)(5-\omega^2) + 8\omega^2}{(5-\omega^2)^2 + 4\omega^2}$$

Numerator simplifies to:

$$\begin{aligned}
(3-\omega^2)(5-\omega^2) + 8\omega^2 &= 15 - 3\omega^2 - 5\omega^2 + \omega^4 + 8\omega^2 \\[4pt]
&= \omega^4 + 0\cdot\omega^2 + 15 = \omega^4 + 15
\end{aligned}$$

$\omega^4 + 15 > 0$ for all $\omega$. Denominator is also positive. So $\text{Re}\,Y(j\omega) > 0$. ✅ **Property 7**

**Conclusion:** $Y(s)$ **is a valid driving-point admittance**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b)

**Derivation of stability conditions:**

A linear time-invariant system with impulse response $h(t)$ is **BIBO stable** if and only if:

$$\int_0^\infty \vert h(t) \vert\, dt < \infty$$

For a network function with partial-fraction expansion $H(s) = \sum_i \frac{K_i}{s - p_i}$, the impulse response is:

$$h(t) = \sum_i K_i\,e^{p_i t}\,u(t)$$

Each term $K_i e^{p_i t}$ is integrable only if $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0$. Therefore, **all poles must lie strictly in the LHP**.

**Routh-Hurwitz Criterion:**

For a polynomial $Q(s) = a_n s^n + a_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_1 s + a_0$ to have all roots with strictly negative real parts:

1. All coefficients $a_i$ must be present (no missing terms).
2. All coefficients must be **positive**.
3. The **Routh array** must have no sign changes in its first column.

**Application to $Q(s) = s^3 + 3s^2 + 4s + 2$:**

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|
| $s^3$ | 1 | 4 | 0 |
| $s^2$ | 3 | 2 | 0 |
| $s^1$ | $(3\cdot 4 - 1\cdot 2)/3 = 10/3$ | 0 | |
| $s^0$ | 2 | | |

First column: $1, 3, 10/3, 2$ — **all positive, no sign change**. ✅

**Conclusion:** $Q(s)$ is **stable**; all roots have negative real parts. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Triggers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting conjugate pairs (Property 2):** If you write the pole locations but write only one complex pole, you lose 1-2 marks. Always explicitly state *"and its conjugate"*.
> 2. **Confusing DPF with TF properties:** A transfer function can have RHP zeros (non-minimum phase) — but a driving-point function CANNOT (positive-realness). Examiners **frequently test this distinction**.
> 3. **Skipping the positive-real test:** Many students verify only Properties 1–4 and stop. KTU examiners specifically allocate **2 marks** to the positive-real condition. If you skip it, you cap at 5/7 even on a correct solution.
> 4. **Routh array arithmetic errors:** One wrong entry in the Routh array invalidates the entire conclusion. Always **double-check the arithmetic**, especially when subtracting products.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Properties of DPF and TF**

- 🔑 **DPF = input and output at the *same* port**; **TF = different ports or points**.
- 🔑 A DPF takes one of two forms: **driving-point impedance** $Z(s)$ or **driving-point admittance** $Y(s) = 1/Z(s)$.
- 🔑 All network functions are **rational functions** $P(s)/Q(s)$ with **real coefficients**.
- 🔑 **Complex poles and zeros must occur in conjugate pairs** — non-negotiable consequence of reality.
- 🔑 **All poles must lie in the LHP** (strictly) for stability; LC networks may have simple $j\omega$-axis poles (lossless resonance).
- 🔑 **No repeated poles on $j\omega$-axis** — would cause $t\sin\omega t$ instability.
- 🔑 **Degree condition:** For DPF admittance, $m \le n$; for DPF impedance of R-L network, $m \ge n$.
- 🔑 **LC networks** have $Z(s)$ as a **ratio of odd/even** polynomials in $s$ (Foster Reactance Theorem).
- 🔑 A valid passive DPF must be a **positive-real function**: $\text{Re}\,Z(j\omega) \ge 0$ for all real $\omega$.
- 🔑 The **Routh-Hurwitz criterion** is the algorithmic shortcut to check pole locations without solving the polynomial.
- 🔑 In KTU exams, always **state which property you are verifying**, then show the math — examiners reward methodology, not just final answers.
- 🔑 Memorize the **seven properties** verbatim; they appear in Part A almost every year.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Significance of Poles and Zeros of Network Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT414 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Significance of Poles and Zeros of Network Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Network Function

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEMRT414 – Network Theory**, a **Network Function** is defined as the ratio of the response (output) phasor to the excitation (input) phasor in the complex frequency domain (s-domain), assuming all initial conditions are set to zero. It is the Laplace-domain equivalent of the frequency response.

$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$$

where:
- $H(s)$ is the Network Function
- $Y(s)$ is the Laplace Transform of the output (response) signal
- $X(s)$ is the Laplace Transform of the input (excitation) signal
- $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is the complex frequency variable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A network function characterizes a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) network completely. The classification depends on the terminals where input and output are measured:
> - **Driving Point Impedance/Admittance** (same port)
> - **Transfer Function** (different ports) — e.g., Voltage Transfer Function $G_{21}(s) = \dfrac{V_2(s)}{V_1(s)}$, Transfer Impedance $Z_{21}(s) = \dfrac{V_2(s)}{I_1(s)}$

### 1.2 Formal Definition of Poles and Zeros

Any rational network function can be expressed in the standard **Pole-Zero Form**:

$$H(s) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{m}(s - z_i)}{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(s - p_j)}$$

- **Zeros** ($z_i$): The finite values of $s$ for which $H(s) = 0$. They are the roots of the numerator polynomial.
- **Poles** ($p_j$): The finite values of $s$ for which $H(s) \to \infty$. They are the roots of the denominator polynomial.
- $K$ is the **Scaling Constant** (or gain factor).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constraint:** For a realizable network function, the order of the numerator $m$ must be **less than or equal to** the order of the denominator $n$, i.e., $m \le n$. If $m > n$, the function represents an **improper** network which is physically unrealizable with passive elements.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Lake Analogy"

Imagine the network function $H(s)$ as the **depth of a lake** mapped over a vast terrain where the horizontal plane represents the complex frequency $s$:

- **Zeros are like "islands"** rising out of the lake. The water level is exactly zero at these points.
- **Poles are like "bottomless whirlpools"** where the lake becomes infinitely deep.
- The **scaling constant $K$** is the average sea level of the lake.
- The **s-plane (terrain)** tells us how the lake behaves at every complex frequency.

Just as a sailor would want to know where the whirlpools and islands are to navigate safely, an engineer plots the **Pole-Zero Plot** to understand whether a network will be stable, oscillate, or explode uncontrollably.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental physical constants relevant in pole-zero analysis are:
> - **Stability Boundary:** The imaginary axis ($j\omega$-axis) of the s-plane, with **Neper frequency** $\sigma = 0$ measured in **Np/s (Nepers per second)**.
> - **Damped Natural Frequency:** $\omega_d$ measured in **rad/s**.
> - **Damping Ratio:** $\zeta$ (dimensionless, $0 \le \zeta \le 1$ for underdamped systems).
> - **Quality Factor:** $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ (dimensionless).
> - **Bandwidth:** $BW$ in **Hz** or **rad/s**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Significance of Poles — The "Natural Behavior" Decoders

The poles of a network function completely govern the **natural (transient) response** of the system. This is the response that arises due to the system's internal energy storage and decays (or grows) over time.

**Why poles matter:**
1. **Stability Determination:** The location of poles in the s-plane directly indicates whether the network is stable, marginally stable, or unstable.
2. **Transient Response Shape:** Poles dictate the type of natural response (exponential decay, damped oscillation, pure oscillation, growth).
3. **Natural Frequencies:** Each pole represents a natural mode of oscillation or decay.

**Pole Location vs. Time Response:**

| Pole Location in s-plane | Time-Domain Behavior $h(t)$ | Physical Meaning |
|:-------------------------|:----------------------------|:-----------------|
| Real negative axis ($p = -\alpha$) | $K e^{-\alpha t}$ | Pure exponential decay |
| Real positive axis ($p = +\alpha$) | $K e^{+\alpha t}$ | Exponential growth (UNSTABLE) |
| Origin ($p = 0$) | $K$ (constant) | Marginal stability |
| Complex pair LHP ($-\alpha \pm j\omega_d$) | $K e^{-\alpha t}\cos(\omega_d t + \phi)$ | Damped oscillation |
| Complex pair RHP ($+\alpha \pm j\omega_d$) | $K e^{+\alpha t}\cos(\omega_d t + \phi)$ | Growing oscillation (UNSTABLE) |
| Imaginary axis ($\pm j\omega_0$) | $K \cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$ | Sustained oscillation (MARGINALLY STABLE) |

### 2.2 Significance of Zeros — The "Spectral Filter" Decoders

Zeros govern the **forced response** and shape the amplitude/phase characteristics of the output.

**Why zeros matter:**
1. **Frequency Response Shaping:** Zeros cause sharp dips (notches) in the magnitude response at specific frequencies.
2. **Coupling Between Modes:** Zeros control how much each natural mode is excited by the input.
3. **Transmission Blockers:** A zero at $s = j\omega_0$ means the network completely blocks that frequency — useful in **notch filters** (e.g., 50 Hz hum rejection in audio).
4. **Time-Domain Overshoot:** Zeros placed close to poles in the LHP can dramatically affect the transient overshoot and settling time.

### 2.3 Stability Criteria (KTU High-Yield Concepts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Rule:** For BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stability of a network function $H(s)$:
> - **Absolutely Stable:** All poles must lie strictly in the **Left-Half Plane (LHP)**. Re$(p_j) < 0 \ \forall j$.
> - **Marginally Stable:** Simple (non-repeated) poles on the $j\omega$-axis; all other poles in LHP.
> - **Unstable:** Any pole in the **Right-Half Plane (RHP)** OR repeated poles on the $j\omega$-axis.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Typical Use |
|:--|:---------------|:------------|:------------|
| 1 | $H(s) = K \cdot \dfrac{\prod_{i=1}^{m}(s - z_i)}{\prod_{j=1}^{n}(s - p_j)}$ | Standard pole-zero form | General network function |
| 2 | $h(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{H(s)\} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} R_j e^{p_j t}$ | Impulse response via residue expansion | Time-domain analysis |
| 3 | $R_j = \lim_{s \to p_j} (s - p_j) H(s)$ | Residue at pole $p_j$ (simple pole) | Partial fraction expansion |
| 4 | $p_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm j\omega_d$ | Complex conjugate pole pair | Second-order systems |
| 5 | $\alpha = \zeta \omega_n$ | Neper frequency in terms of damping | Standard form mapping |
| 6 | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}$ | Damped natural frequency | Underdamped case |
| 7 | $Q = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta} = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\alpha}$ | Quality factor | Resonant systems |
| 8 | $H(j\omega) = K \cdot \dfrac{\prod (j\omega - z_i)}{\prod (j\omega - p_j)}$ | Frequency response substitution $s = j\omega$ | Magnitude/phase plots |
| 9 | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert H(j\omega) \vert$ | Bode magnitude in dB | Frequency domain |
| 10 | $m \le n$ for physical realizability | Properness condition | Network synthesis |
| 11 | $\angle H(j\omega) = \sum \angle(j\omega - z_i) - \sum \angle(j\omega - p_j)$ | Phase response | Bode phase plot |
| 12 | $BW = \omega_n \left[\zeta \pm \sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1 + \sqrt{2(1-2\zeta^2) + 1/\zeta^2}}\right] \cdot \ldots$ | Bandwidth (simplified: $BW \approx \omega_n$ for high Q) | Resonant response |

> **Note:** The symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes magnitude, written with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pole-zero analysis is foundational in:
- **Filter Design** (Low-pass, High-pass, Band-pass, Band-stop): Butterworth, Chebyshev, Bessel filter placement of poles.
- **Control Systems:** Root locus technique for compensator design.
- **Antenna Matching Networks:** Impedance matching by pole-zero placement.
- **Audio Equalizers:** Notch filters to remove 50/60 Hz hum.
- **Oscillator Design:** Placing poles on the $j\omega$-axis to generate sustained oscillations (e.g., Wien Bridge Oscillator).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Poles to Time-Domain Impulse Response

Consider a network function with two distinct simple poles at $p_1$ and $p_2$:

$$H(s) = \frac{K}{(s - p_1)(s - p_2)}$$

**Step 1:** Apply partial fraction expansion to decompose $H(s)$ into simpler terms.

$$H(s) = \frac{R_1}{s - p_1} + \frac{R_2}{s - p_2}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the residue $R_1$ by multiplying both sides by $(s - p_1)$ and taking the limit $s \to p_1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \lim_{s \to p_1} (s - p_1) \cdot \frac{K}{(s - p_1)(s - p_2)} \\
&= \lim_{s \to p_1} \frac{K}{s - p_2} \\
&= \frac{K}{p_1 - p_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Similarly, compute $R_2$ by symmetry of operation:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_2 &= \lim_{s \to p_2} (s - p_2) \cdot \frac{K}{(s - p_1)(s - p_2)} \\
&= \lim_{s \to p_2} \frac{K}{s - p_1} \\
&= \frac{K}{p_2 - p_1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the residues back into the partial fraction expansion:

$$H(s) = \frac{K}{p_1 - p_2} \cdot \frac{1}{s - p_1} + \frac{K}{p_2 - p_1} \cdot \frac{1}{s - p_2}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the inverse Laplace transform using the standard pair $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s - a}\right\} = e^{at} u(t)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
h(t) &= \frac{K}{p_1 - p_2} e^{p_1 t} + \frac{K}{p_2 - p_1} e^{p_2 t} \\
&= \frac{K}{p_1 - p_2} \left[ e^{p_1 t} - e^{p_2 t} \right] u(t)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Stability Interpretation:**
- If $p_1$ and $p_2$ are both in the LHP (Re$(p_1) < 0$ and Re$(p_2) < 0$), then both exponentials decay as $t \to \infty$, giving $h(t) \to 0$. **System is stable.**
- If either pole is in the RHP, the corresponding exponential grows without bound. **System is unstable.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Time Response from a Complex Conjugate Pole Pair

Let the network function have complex conjugate poles at $p_{1,2} = -\alpha \pm j\omega_d$ and no finite zeros:

$$H(s) = \frac{K}{(s + \alpha - j\omega_d)(s + \alpha + j\omega_d)} = \frac{K}{(s + \alpha)^2 + \omega_d^2}$$

**Step 1:** Recognize that $(s + \alpha)^2 + \omega_d^2$ can be matched to the standard Laplace pair.

Recall the standard form: $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega_d t)\} = \dfrac{\omega_d}{(s + \alpha)^2 + \omega_d^2}$

**Step 2:** Rewrite $H(s)$ to match the standard form:

$$H(s) = \frac{K}{\omega_d} \cdot \frac{\omega_d}{(s + \alpha)^2 + \omega_d^2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the inverse Laplace transform:

$$h(t) = \frac{K}{\omega_d} e^{-\alpha t} \sin(\omega_d t) \cdot u(t)$$

**Step 4: Physical Interpretation:**
- The exponential envelope $e^{-\alpha t}$ is controlled by the **real part** of the pole ($-\alpha$).
- The oscillation frequency $\omega_d$ is controlled by the **imaginary part** of the pole.
- If $\alpha > 0$ (LHP), oscillations decay — **damped oscillation**.
- Settling time (to 2% of final value): $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\alpha} = \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$
- Number of oscillations: related to $\omega_d$ vs $\alpha$ ratio.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Steady-State Sinusoidal Response

For a stable network function $H(s)$ with all poles in the LHP, the steady-state response to a sinusoidal input $x(t) = X_m \cos(\omega_0 t)$ is:

$$y_{ss}(t) = X_m \vert H(j\omega_0) \vert \cos(\omega_0 t + \angle H(j\omega_0) + \phi)$$

**Derivation Outline:**

**Step 1:** Input in s-domain: $X(s) = \dfrac{X_m s}{s^2 + \omega_0^2}$

**Step 2:** Output in s-domain: $Y(s) = H(s) \cdot X(s) = H(s) \cdot \dfrac{X_m s}{(s - j\omega_0)(s + j\omega_0)}$

**Step 3:** Partial fraction expansion produces terms with poles at $p_j$ (system poles) and at $\pm j\omega_0$ (input poles).

$$Y(s) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{R_j}{s - p_j} + \frac{A}{s - j\omega_0} + \frac{A^*}{s + j\omega_0}$$

**Step 4:** As $t \to \infty$, the system poles contribute terms $e^{p_j t}$. For stable systems, Re$(p_j) < 0$, so these vanish.

**Step 5:** The remaining terms are purely oscillatory and constitute the steady-state response:

$$y_{ss}(t) = A e^{j\omega_0 t} + A^* e^{-j\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 6:** Substituting $A = \dfrac{X_m (j\omega_0) H(j\omega_0)}{2 j\omega_0} = \dfrac{X_m H(j\omega_0)}{2}$ and applying Euler's identity:

$$y_{ss}(t) = X_m \vert H(j\omega_0) \vert \cos(\omega_0 t + \angle H(j\omega_0))$$

**Conclusion:** The steady-state sinusoidal response is found by simply evaluating the network function at $s = j\omega_0$ — this is the **Phasor Transform** principle.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pole-Zero → Time Response Summary

| Network Function Form | Impulse Response $h(t)$ | Stability |
|:----------------------|:------------------------|:----------|
| $\dfrac{K}{s + a}$ | $K e^{-at} u(t)$ | Stable if $a > 0$ |
| $\dfrac{K}{s - a}$ | $K e^{at} u(t)$ | Unstable if $a > 0$ |
| $\dfrac{K}{s}$ | $K \cdot u(t)$ (step) | Marginal |
| $\dfrac{K}{(s+a)^2}$ | $K t e^{-at} u(t)$ | Stable if $a > 0$ |
| $\dfrac{K}{(s+a)^2 + \omega_d^2}$ | $\dfrac{K}{\omega_d} e^{-at} \sin(\omega_d t) u(t)$ | Stable if $a > 0$ |
| $\dfrac{K(s+c)}{s+a}$ | $K\delta(t) + K(c-a)e^{-at} u(t)$ | Stable if $a > 0$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Network Function Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal x of t] --> B[Laplace Transform X of s]
    B --> C[Apply Network Function H of s]
    C --> D[Output Signal Y of s = H of s times X of s]
    D --> E[Partial Fraction Expansion]
    E --> F1[Residue at Poles p sub j]
    E --> F2[Residue at Zeros z sub i]
    F1 --> G1[Exponential Modes e to the p sub j t]
    F2 --> G2[Coupling Coefficients]
    G1 --> H[Time Domain Response y of t]
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[Stability and Frequency Analysis]

    stepA[Step A: Time Domain Input] --> A
    stepB[Step B: s-Domain Transformation] --> B
    stepC[Step C: System Action] --> C
    stepD[Step D: s-Domain Output] --> D
    stepE[Step E: Decomposition] --> E
```

### 4.2 S-Plane Pole-Zero Plot Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPlane[S Plane Layout]
        axis[Imaginary Axis j omega] --- origin[Origin 0 0]
        LHP[Left Half Plane sigma less than 0]
        RHP[Right Half Plane sigma greater than 0]
        LHP --- axis
        axis --- RHP
    end

    P1[Real Pole at minus alpha] --> LHP
    P2[Real Pole at plus alpha] --> RHP
    P3[Complex Pole minus alpha plus j omega sub d] --> LHP
    P4[Complex Pole plus alpha plus j omega sub d] --> RHP
    P5[Imaginary Pole plus j omega sub 0] --> axis
    P6[Imaginary Pole minus j omega sub 0] --> axis
    Z1[Zero at minus z sub 1] --> LHP
    Z2[Zero at j omega sub n] --> axis

    LHPNote[Stable Region Poles Here] --> LHP
    RHPNote[Unstable Region Poles Here] --> RHP
    axisNote[Marginally Stable Region] --> axis
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Stability Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify All Poles of H of s] --> Check1{All poles in Left Half Plane?}
    Check1 -- Yes --> Stable1[System is BIBO Stable]
    Check1 -- No --> Check2{Any pole in Right Half Plane?}
    Check2 -- Yes --> Unstable1[System is Unstable Output grows]
    Check2 -- No --> Check3{Simple poles on j omega axis?}
    Check3 -- Yes --> Marginal[System is Marginally Stable Sustained Oscillation]
    Check3 -- No --> Unstable2[System is Unstable Repeated poles on j omega axis]
    Stable1 --> End[Final Stability Verdict]
    Unstable1 --> End
    Marginal --> End
    Unstable2 --> End
```

### 4.4 Diagram: First-Order RC Low-Pass Filter Pole-Zero Plot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RC[RC Low Pass Network] --> Transfer[Transfer Function H of s equals 1 over 1 plus sRC]
    Transfer --> Pole[Pole Location minus 1 over RC]
    Transfer --> NoZero[No Finite Zeros]
    Pole --> LHPPlot[Pole Plotted on Negative Real Axis of s Plane]
    LHPPlot --> Stability[System is Stable Time Constant tau equals RC]
    NoZero --> FreqResp[Flat DC Gain Unity Attenuation above cutoff]
```
