## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
*What is meant by interrelationship among two-port network parameters? Why is it necessary in network analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Interrelationship among two-port network parameters refers to the mathematical conversion formulas that allow transformation of one set of two-port parameters (Z, Y, h, g, ABCD, A'B'C'D') into another. It is necessary because different parameter sets are convenient for different applications: Z-parameters for series-connected networks, Y-parameters for parallel-connected networks, h-parameters for transistor modelling, and ABCD-parameters for cascaded networks. Using interrelationship formulas, the engineer can pick the most mathematically convenient parameter set for analysis while keeping the physical description of the network intact.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*For a reciprocal two-port network, state the interrelationships among the Z, Y, h, and ABCD parameters.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a reciprocal two-port network:
- $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ (open-circuit reciprocity)
- $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ (short-circuit reciprocity)
- $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ (hybrid reciprocity)
- $AD - BC = 1$ (transmission reciprocity)
- $A' D' - B' C' = 1$ (inverse transmission reciprocity)

These reduce the number of independent parameters from 4 to 3.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign convention trap in ABCD:** $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$ (note the **minus** sign before $B I_2$). Many students write $V_1 = A V_2 + B I_2$ — that loses **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Determinant confusion:** $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}$. Writing it as $Z_{11} Z_{12} - Z_{21} Z_{22}$ is **wrong** — diagonal terms are multiplied, not off-diagonal.
> 3. **Reciprocity assumption trap:** Do **not** assume $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ unless the problem explicitly says the network is reciprocal/passive. If dependent sources are present, this is **not** valid.
> 4. **Units trap:** $A$ and $D$ are dimensionless, $B$ has units of **Ohms**, $C$ has units of **Siemens**. $h_{11}$ is in **Ohms**, $h_{22}$ in **Siemens**, $h_{12}$ and $h_{21}$ are **dimensionless**. Wrong unit identification = **-1 mark**.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **Y-parameters in terms of Z-parameters** for a two-port network. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the two-port network shown in the figure (assume a T-network with series impedance $Z_a$ in the top arm, $Z_b$ in the series output arm, and $Z_c$ as the shunt arm), determine the **Z-parameters** and hence compute the **ABCD-parameters** using the standard interrelationship formulas. Assume $Z_a = 2\,\Omega$, $Z_b = 3\,\Omega$, $Z_c = 5\,\Omega$. (7 marks)

---

#### Question A — Model Solution

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the Z-parameter equations:**
$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2 \quad (1)$$
$$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2 \quad (2)$$

**Step 2 — Express in matrix form:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — To obtain Y-parameters, we need $I_1, I_2$ as functions of $V_1, V_2$.** Pre-multiply by the inverse of the Z-matrix:

$$Z^{-1} = \frac{1}{Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}} \begin{bmatrix} Z_{22} & -Z_{12} \\ -Z_{21} & Z_{11} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the determinant:** $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}$.

**Step 5 — Therefore:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\Delta Z} \begin{bmatrix} Z_{22} & -Z_{12} \\ -Z_{21} & Z_{11} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Comparing with $I_i = Y_{i1} V_1 + Y_{i2} V_2$:**

$$\boxed{Y_{11} = \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{12} = \frac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{21} = \frac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{22} = \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z}}$$

This is the complete derivation (7 marks).

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

For a T-network with $Z_a$ (series input), $Z_c$ (shunt), $Z_b$ (series output):

**Step 1 — Compute Z-parameters directly:**
- $Z_{11} = $ (input impedance with output open) $= Z_a + Z_c = 2 + 5 = 7\,\Omega$
- $Z_{22} = $ (output impedance with input open) $= Z_b + Z_c = 3 + 5 = 8\,\Omega$
- $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = $ (transfer impedance with output open) $= Z_c = 5\,\Omega$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta Z$:**
$$\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21} = (7)(8) - (5)(5) = 56 - 25 = 31$$

**Step 3 — Use the interrelationship formulas (Z → ABCD):**
$$A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{7}{5} = 1.4$$
$$B = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}} = \frac{31}{5} = 6.2\,\Omega$$
$$C = \frac{1}{Z_{21}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2\,\text{S}$$
$$D = \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{8}{5} = 1.6$$

**Step 4 — Verification using reciprocity:** $AD - BC = (1.4)(1.6) - (6.2)(0.2) = 2.24 - 1.24 = 1.00$ ✓ (confirms T-network is reciprocal, as expected).

**Final ABCD matrix:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.4 & 6.2\,\Omega \\ 0.2\,\text{S} & 1.6 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **ABCD-parameters in terms of h-parameters** for a two-port network. (7 marks)

**(b)** The h-parameters of a transistor in common-emitter configuration are given as: $h_{11} = 1100\,\Omega$, $h_{12} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$, $h_{21} = 50$, $h_{22} = 25 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{S}$. Determine the **Y-parameters** and **Z-parameters** of the transistor using the standard interrelationship formulas. (7 marks)

---

#### Question B — Model Solution

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the h-parameter equations:**
$$V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2 \quad (1)$$
$$I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2 \quad (2)$$

**Step 2 — Target: ABCD form** $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$ and $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$.

**Step 3 — Solve (2) for $I_1$:**
$$I_1 = \frac{I_2 - h_{22} V_2}{h_{21}} = -\frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}} V_2 + \frac{1}{h_{21}} I_2$$

**Step 4 — Compare with $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$:** So $C = -\dfrac{h_{22}}{h_{21}}$ and $D = -\dfrac{1}{h_{21}}$.

**Step 5 — Substitute $I_1$ in equation (1):**
$$V_1 = h_{11}\left(-\frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}} V_2 + \frac{1}{h_{21}} I_2\right) + h_{12} V_2$$

$$V_1 = \left(h_{12} - \frac{h_{11} h_{22}}{h_{21}}\right) V_2 + \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}} I_2$$

$$V_1 = \frac{h_{12} h_{21} - h_{11} h_{22}}{h_{21}} V_2 + \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}} I_2$$

$$V_1 = -\frac{\Delta h}{h_{21}} V_2 - \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}} I_2$$

**Step 6 — Compare with $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$:** So $A = -\dfrac{\Delta h}{h_{21}}$ and $B = -\dfrac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}$.

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{A = \frac{-\Delta h}{h_{21}}, \quad B = \frac{-h_{11}}{h_{21}}, \quad C = \frac{-h_{22}}{h_{21}}, \quad D = \frac{-1}{h_{21}}}$$

where $\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21}$. (7 marks)

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta h$:**
$$\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = (1100)(25 \times 10^{-6}) - (2.5 \times 10^{-4})(50)$$
$$= 0.0275 - 0.0125 = 0.015$$

**Step 2 — Convert h to Z (using interrelationship formulas):**

From the conversion table:
- $Z_{11} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h_{22}} = \dfrac{0.015}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 600\,\Omega$
- $Z_{12} = \dfrac{h_{12}}{h_{22}} = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 10\,\Omega$
- $Z_{21} = \dfrac{-h_{21}}{h_{22}} = \dfrac{-50}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = -2 \times 10^{6}\,\Omega = -2\,\text{M}\Omega$
- $Z_{22} = \dfrac{1}{h_{22}} = \dfrac{1}{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 40{,}000\,\Omega = 40\,\text{k}\Omega$

**Step 3 — Convert h to Y (alternative route: convert h to Z, then Z to Y):**

Compute $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}$:
$$\Delta Z = (600)(40{,}000) - (10)(-2 \times 10^{6}) = 24 \times 10^{6} + 20 \times 10^{6} = 44 \times 10^{6}$$

Then:
- $Y_{11} = \dfrac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z} = \dfrac{40{,}000}{44 \times 10^{6}} = 9.09 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{S} = 0.909\,\text{mS}$
- $Y_{12} = \dfrac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z} = \dfrac{-10}{44 \times 10^{6}} = -2.27 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{S} = -0.227\,\mu\text{S}$
- $Y_{21} = \dfrac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{6}}{44 \times 10^{6}} = 0.0455\,\text{S} = 45.5\,\text{mS}$
- $Y_{22} = \dfrac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z} = \dfrac{600}{44 \times 10^{6}} = 1.36 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{S} = 13.6\,\mu\text{S}$

**Final Y-matrix:**
$$Y = \begin{bmatrix} 0.909\,\text{mS} & -0.227\,\mu\text{S} \\ 45.5\,\text{mS} & 13.6\,\mu\text{S} \end{bmatrix}$$

(Note: $Y_{12} \neq Y_{21}$, confirming the transistor is **non-reciprocal** because it contains a dependent source — a classic KTU-level insight worth 1 extra mark.)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Points for Last-Minute KTU Revision**

- ✅ **Two-port networks** have 4 variables ($V_1, I_1, V_2, I_2$). Six parameter sets arise by choosing different pairs as **independent** variables.
- ✅ **Z-parameters** — independent currents $I_1, I_2$. Found by **open-circuit** tests. $V = ZI$.
- ✅ **Y-parameters** — independent voltages $V_1, V_2$. Found by **short-circuit** tests. $I = YV$.
- ✅ **h-parameters** — mixed: $I_1$ and $V_2$ independent. Heavily used in **transistor modelling** (low-frequency hybrid-π model).
- ✅ **g-parameters** — inverse of h: $V_1$ and $I_2$ independent.
- ✅ **ABCD-parameters** — output variables independent. **Matrices multiply** for cascaded networks — this is the #1 reason for using them.
- ✅ **A'B'C'D'** — inverse transmission. $A'D' - B'C' = 1$ for reciprocal case.
- ✅ **Universal relation:** $Y = Z^{-1}$ and $Z = Y^{-1}$.
- ✅ **Reciprocity conditions** (passive, bilateral network):
  - $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$, $h_{12} = -h_{21}$, $g_{12} = -g_{21}$, $AD - BC = 1$, $A' D' - B' C' = 1$.
- ✅ **Symmetry conditions** (input = output under port swap):
  - $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$, $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$, $\Delta h = 1$, $\Delta g = 1$, $A = D$, $A' = D'$.
- ✅ **Sign convention** in ABCD: $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$, $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$ — note the **minus signs**.
- ✅ **Units check:** $A, D, h_{12}, h_{21}, g_{12}, g_{21}, A', D'$ are dimensionless. $B$ is in Ohms, $C$ in Siemens, $h_{11}$ in Ohms, $h_{22}$ in Siemens, $g_{11}$ in Siemens, $g_{22}$ in Ohms, $B'$ in Ohms, $C'$ in Siemens.
- ✅ **Determinant shorthand:** $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}$ — always diagonal product minus off-diagonal product.
- ✅ **Conversion strategy in exams:** Always start from the *source* matrix equation, eliminate the unwanted variables to get the *target* form, then read off coefficients.
- ✅ **Most commonly asked KTU conversion:** Z → Y, Z → h, Z → ABCD, h → ABCD, ABCD → A'B'C'D'.
- ✅ **Cascade trick:** $T_{\text{overall}} = T_1 \cdot T_2 \cdot T_3 \cdots$ (order matters — left-to-right cascade).
- ✅ **Series-parallel connection rules:** Series → add Z-matrices. Parallel → add Y-matrices.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series and Parallel Connections of Two Port Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT414 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Series and Parallel Connections of Two Port Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4: TWO-PORT NETWORK INTERCONNECTION STRUCTURES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Two-Port Network Interconnection

A **Two-Port Network** is an electrical network encapsulated within a closed boundary, accessible through exactly two pairs of terminals: the input port (1-1') and the output port (2-2'). When multiple two-port networks are interconnected, the resulting overall network can often be described simply by adding or multiplying their respective parameter matrices, depending on the topology of the connection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A two-port network is said to be connected in **series** when the currents entering port 1 of network A equal the current entering port 1 of network B, and similarly for port 2. A two-port network is said to be connected in **parallel** when the voltage across port 1 of network A equals the voltage across port 1 of network B, and similarly for port 2.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "Garden Hose and Sprinkler System"**
>
> Imagine you connect two garden hoses (representing two-port networks) end-to-end. Water flowing through them experiences resistance from BOTH hoses combined. This is the **series connection** — effects (impedance) **add up**.
>
> Now imagine two sprinklers connected to a Y-junction pipe. The water pressure at the junction is the SAME for both sprinklers, but the water SPLITS between them. The total current is the sum of individual currents. This is the **parallel connection** — admittances **add up**.
>
> **Key Insight:** Just like resistors add in series (R₁ + R₂) but conductances add in parallel (G₁ + G₂), two-port network parameters follow the same pattern based on which physical quantity (voltage or current) is shared.

### 1.3 Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

- **Impedance (Z)**: Measured in **Ohms (Ω)** — opposition to current flow
- **Admittance (Y)**: Measured in **Siemens (S)** — ease of current flow
- **Hybrid Parameters (h, g)**: Dimensionless ratios
- **Transmission Parameters (ABCD)**: Dimensionless ratios used in cascade analysis
- **Port Currents**: I₁, I₂ in **Amperes (A)**
- **Port Voltages**: V₁, V₂ in **Volts (V)**

### 1.4 Why Interconnection Theory Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Complex circuits (transistor amplifiers, filters, transmission lines) are routinely broken into cascaded sub-networks for analysis. Engineers use interconnection rules to:
> 1. **Design modular systems** (build blocks and connect them)
> 2. **Simplify analysis** (analyze small sub-networks, combine results)
> 3. **Verify designs** (compare measured vs. predicted composite behavior)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Series Connection of Two-Port Networks

### 2.1.1 Definition and Topology

Two two-port networks **N_a** and **N_b** are connected in **series** when:
- Port 1 of N_a is connected in series with port 1 of N_b
- Port 2 of N_a is connected in series with port 2 of N_b

This means the **same current** flows through both port 1 terminals, and the **same current** flows through both port 2 terminals.

### 2.1.2 Physical Conditions for Valid Series Connection

For a series connection to be valid and representable by simple parameter addition:

1. **Port Current Equality**: I₁ᵃ = I₁ᵇ and I₂ᵃ = I₂ᵇ
2. **Voltage Additivity**: V₁ = V₁ᵃ + V₁ᵇ and V₂ = V₂ᵃ + V₂ᵇ
3. **No Interaction**: Currents entering port 1' of one network must NOT affect the other network's terminal conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Z-parameters are used for series connection:**
> Z-parameters express port voltages in terms of port currents:
> V₁ = Z₁₁·I₁ + Z₁₂·I₂
> V₂ = Z₂₁·I₁ + Z₂₂·I₂
> Since currents are SHARED in series and voltages ADD, Z-parameters naturally add.

### 2.1.3 Derivation Logic of Z-Parameter Addition

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1:** For network N_a, write Z-parameter equations:
  V₁ᵃ = Z₁₁ᵃ·I₁ + Z₁₂ᵃ·I₂
  V₂ᵃ = Z₂₁ᵃ·I₁ + Z₂₂ᵃ·I₂

- **Step 2:** For network N_b, write Z-parameter equations:
  V₁ᵇ = Z₁₁ᵇ·I₁ + Z₁₂ᵇ·I₂
  V₂ᵇ = Z₂₁ᵇ·I₁ + Z₂₂ᵇ·I₂

- **Step 3:** Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) — voltages add in series:
  V₁ = V₁ᵃ + V₁ᵇ
  V₂ = V₂ᵃ + V₂ᵇ

- **Step 4:** Substitute and collect terms to obtain the overall Z-matrix.

### 2.2 Parallel Connection of Two-Port Networks

### 2.2.1 Definition and Topology

Two two-port networks **N_a** and **N_b** are connected in **parallel** when:
- Port 1 of N_a is in parallel with port 1 of N_b
- Port 2 of N_a is in parallel with port 2 of N_b

This means the **same voltage** appears across both port 1 terminals, and the **same voltage** appears across both port 2 terminals.

### 2.2.2 Physical Conditions for Valid Parallel Connection

1. **Port Voltage Equality**: V₁ᵃ = V₁ᵇ = V₁ and V₂ᵃ = V₂ᵇ = V₂
2. **Current Additivity**: I₁ = I₁ᵃ + I₁ᵇ and I₂ = I₂ᵃ + I₂ᵇ
3. **No Cross-Coupling**: Voltages across the ports must be the only shared quantities

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Y-parameters are used for parallel connection:**
> Y-parameters express port currents in terms of port voltages:
> I₁ = Y₁₁·V₁ + Y₁₂·V₂
> I₂ = Y₂₁·V₁ + Y₂₂·V₂
> Since voltages are SHARED in parallel and currents ADD, Y-parameters naturally add.

### 2.2.3 Derivation Logic of Y-Parameter Addition

- **Step 1:** For network N_a: I₁ᵃ = Y₁₁ᵃ·V₁ + Y₁₂ᵃ·V₂
- **Step 2:** For network N_b: I₁ᵇ = Y₁₁ᵇ·V₁ + Y₁₂ᵇ·V₂
- **Step 3:** Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) — currents add in parallel:
  I₁ = I₁ᵃ + I₁ᵇ
- **Step 4:** Combine to get the overall Y-matrix.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for KTU exam problems on this topic.**

| Connection Type | Parameter Used | Combination Rule | Condition for Validity |
|-----------------|----------------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Series** | **Z-parameters** (Impedance) | $[Z] = [Z_a] + [Z_b]$ | Port currents must be equal: I₁ᵃ = I₁ᵇ, I₂ᵃ = I₂ᵇ |
| **Parallel** | **Y-parameters** (Admittance) | $[Y] = [Y_a] + [Y_b]$ | Port voltages must be equal: V₁ᵃ = V₁ᵇ, V₂ᵃ = V₂ᵇ |
| **Cascade (Chain)** | **T-parameters (ABCD)** | $[T] = [T_a] \cdot [T_b]$ | Output of N_a feeds input of N_b |
| **Series-Parallel** | **h-parameters** | $[h] = [h_a] + [h_b]$ | Input in series, output in parallel |
| **Parallel-Series** | **g-parameters** | $[g] = [g_a] + [g_b]$ | Input in parallel, output in series |

### 2.3.1 Matrix Form Representations

**Z-Parameter Matrix (Series Combination):**

$$
[Z_{total}] = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11}^a + Z_{11}^b & Z_{12}^a + Z_{12}^b \\ Z_{21}^a + Z_{21}^b & Z_{22}^a + Z_{22}^b \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Y-Parameter Matrix (Parallel Combination):**

$$
[Y_{total}] = \begin{bmatrix} Y_{11}^a + Y_{11}^b & Y_{12}^a + Y_{12}^b \\ Y_{21}^a + Y_{21}^b & Y_{22}^a + Y_{22}^b \end{bmatrix}
$$

### 2.3.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Interconnection Used | Reason |
|------------------|----------------------|--------|
| **Cascaded Amplifier Design** | Cascade (T-parameters) | Each stage amplifies signal progressively |
| **Transmission Line Cascades** | Cascade (T-parameters) | Lines are joined end-to-end |
| **Parallel Filter Banks** | Parallel (Y-parameters) | Filters share common input voltage |
| **Power Distribution Networks** | Series-Parallel (h-parameters) | Input currents summed, output voltages share load |
| **Modular Transistor Circuits** | Various | Transistors treated as 2-port building blocks |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Series Connection (Z-Parameter Addition)

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Z-Parameters of Individual Networks

**Network A** (described by Z-parameters):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1^a &= Z_{11}^a \cdot I_1 + Z_{12}^a \cdot I_2 \\
V_2^a &= Z_{21}^a \cdot I_1 + Z_{22}^a \cdot I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Network B** (described by Z-parameters):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1^b &= Z_{11}^b \cdot I_1 + Z_{12}^b \cdot I_2 \\
V_2^b &= Z_{21}^b \cdot I_1 + Z_{22}^b \cdot I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Apply Series Connection Conditions

In series connection, the same current $I_1$ enters port 1 of both networks, and the same current $I_2$ enters port 2 of both networks. By KVL, the total port voltage is the algebraic sum of individual port voltages.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= V_1^a + V_1^b \\
V_2 &= V_2^a + V_2^b
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Substitute and Combine

Substituting the individual network equations into the KVL expressions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= (Z_{11}^a \cdot I_1 + Z_{12}^a \cdot I_2) + (Z_{11}^b \cdot I_1 + Z_{12}^b \cdot I_2) \\
V_1 &= (Z_{11}^a + Z_{11}^b) \cdot I_1 + (Z_{12}^a + Z_{12}^b) \cdot I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2 &= (Z_{21}^a \cdot I_1 + Z_{22}^a \cdot I_2) + (Z_{21}^b \cdot I_1 + Z_{22}^b \cdot I_2) \\
V_2 &= (Z_{21}^a + Z_{21}^b) \cdot I_1 + (Z_{22}^a + Z_{22}^b) \cdot I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Express in Standard Z-Parameter Form

Comparing with the standard form $V_1 = Z_{11} \cdot I_1 + Z_{12} \cdot I_2$ and $V_2 = Z_{21} \cdot I_1 + Z_{22} \cdot I_2$, we identify the overall Z-parameters as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{11} &= Z_{11}^a + Z_{11}^b \\
Z_{12} &= Z_{12}^a + Z_{12}^b \\
Z_{21} &= Z_{21}^a + Z_{21}^b \\
Z_{22} &= Z_{22}^a + Z_{22}^b
\end{aligned}
$$

**In compact matrix form:**

$$
[Z] = [Z_a] + [Z_b]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** The Z-parameter formulation naturally respects the "shared current, added voltage" structure of series connection. The matrix addition is element-wise.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Parallel Connection (Y-Parameter Addition)

### 3.2.1 Starting Point — Y-Parameters of Individual Networks

**Network A** (described by Y-parameters):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1^a &= Y_{11}^a \cdot V_1 + Y_{12}^a \cdot V_2 \\
I_2^a &= Y_{21}^a \cdot V_1 + Y_{22}^a \cdot V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Network B** (described by Y-parameters):

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1^b &= Y_{11}^b \cdot V_1 + Y_{12}^b \cdot V_2 \\
I_2^b &= Y_{21}^b \cdot V_1 + Y_{22}^b \cdot V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Apply Parallel Connection Conditions

In parallel connection, both networks share the same port voltages. By KCL, the total port current is the sum of individual network currents.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= I_1^a + I_1^b \\
I_2 &= I_2^a + I_2^b
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Substitute and Combine

Substituting the Y-parameter equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= (Y_{11}^a \cdot V_1 + Y_{12}^a \cdot V_2) + (Y_{11}^b \cdot V_1 + Y_{12}^b \cdot V_2) \\
I_1 &= (Y_{11}^a + Y_{11}^b) \cdot V_1 + (Y_{12}^a + Y_{12}^b) \cdot V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2 &= (Y_{21}^a \cdot V_1 + Y_{22}^a \cdot V_2) + (Y_{21}^b \cdot V_1 + Y_{22}^b \cdot V_2) \\
I_2 &= (Y_{21}^a + Y_{21}^b) \cdot V_1 + (Y_{22}^a + Y_{22}^b) \cdot V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Express in Standard Y-Parameter Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{11} &= Y_{11}^a + Y_{11}^b \\
Y_{12} &= Y_{12}^a + Y_{12}^b \\
Y_{21} &= Y_{21}^a + Y_{21}^b \\
Y_{22} &= Y_{22}^a + Y_{22}^b
\end{aligned}
$$

**In compact matrix form:**

$$
[Y] = [Y_a] + [Y_b]
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example: Series Connection of Two T-Networks

**Problem Setup:** Two identical T-networks (Network A and Network B) with the following Z-parameters are connected in series:

$$
[Z_a] = [Z_b] = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 5 \\ 5 & 15 \end{bmatrix} \, \Omega
$$

**Find the overall Z-parameters.**

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the connection type — **Series** → use Z-parameter addition.

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$
[Z_{total}] = [Z_a] + [Z_b]
$$

**Step 3:** Perform element-wise addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{11} &= 10 + 10 = 20 \, \Omega \\
Z_{12} &= 5 + 5 = 10 \, \Omega \\
Z_{21} &= 5 + 5 = 10 \, \Omega \\
Z_{22} &= 15 + 15 = 30 \, \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Final overall Z-matrix:

$$
[Z_{total}] = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 10 \\ 10 & 30 \end{bmatrix} \, \Omega
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the reciprocity preservation:** Since both original networks were reciprocal (Z₁₂ = Z₂₁), the combined network is also reciprocal (10 = 10). This is a key property maintained under series connection of reciprocal networks.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Parallel Connection of Two π-Networks

**Problem Setup:** Two identical π-networks with the following Y-parameters are connected in parallel:

$$
[Y_a] = [Y_b] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2 & -0.05 \\ -0.05 & 0.3 \end{bmatrix} \, S
$$

**Find the overall Y-parameters.**

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify the connection type — **Parallel** → use Y-parameter addition.

**Step 2:** Apply the formula:

$$
[Y_{total}] = [Y_a] + [Y_b]
$$

**Step 3:** Perform element-wise addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{11} &= 0.2 + 0.2 = 0.4 \, S \\
Y_{12} &= -0.05 + (-0.05) = -0.1 \, S \\
Y_{21} &= -0.05 + (-0.05) = -0.1 \, S \\
Y_{22} &= 0.3 + 0.3 = 0.6 \, S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Final overall Y-matrix:

$$
[Y_{total}] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & -0.1 \\ -0.1 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} \, S
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Port Network Block Representation

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the general structure of a two-port network, clearly showing the four terminals, two ports, and the port variables (voltages and currents).

```mermaid
graph LR
    input1((Terminal 1)) --> node1[Port 1]
    input2((Terminal 1 prime)) --> node1
    node1 --> internalBlock[Two Port Network N]
    internalBlock --> node2[Port 2]
    node2 --> output1((Terminal 2))
    node2 --> output2((Terminal 2 prime))
    node1 -.- v1Label[V1, I1]
    node2 -.- v2Label[V2, I2]
    classDef portStyle fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef termStyle fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class node1,node2 portStyle
    class input1,input2,output1,output2 termStyle
```

## 4.2 Series Connection Architecture

The series connection topology is depicted below. Notice how the two networks are connected such that the same current flows through their respective ports, while their voltages add.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph extCircuit[External Circuit]
        src1((I1 in)) --> seriesNode1
        seriesNode1 --> na1[Network A Port 1]
        seriesNode1 --> nb1[Network B Port 1]
        na1 --> seriesNode2
        nb1 --> seriesNode2
        seriesNode2 --> mid1((Connection point))
    end
    na1 -.-> na2[Network A Port 2]
    nb1 -.-> nb2[Network B Port 2]
    na2 --> seriesNode3
    nb2 --> seriesNode3
    seriesNode3 --> load1((I2 out to load))
    classDef netStyle fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef nodeStyle fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class na1,nb1,na2,nb2 netStyle
    class seriesNode1,seriesNode2,seriesNode3 nodeStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Note:** The key visual signature of a series connection is that the two networks are STACKED along the current path. The same current must pass through both, while their individual voltages sum to the total port voltage.

## 4.3 Parallel Connection Architecture

The parallel connection topology is shown below. The defining feature is that both networks share the same pair of input nodes and the same pair of output nodes.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph parTop[Top Rail]
        top1((Top Node P1)) --> top2((Top Node P2))
    end
    subgraph parBot[Bottom Rail]
        bot1((Bottom Node P1 prime)) --> bot2((Bottom Node P2 prime))
    end
    top1 --- na1[Network A]
    top1 --- nb1[Network B]
    bot1 --- na1
    bot1 --- nb1
    top2 --- na2[Network A output]
    top2 --- nb2[Network B output]
    bot2 --- na2
    bot2 --- nb2
    src((Voltage source V1, V2)) --- top1
    src --- bot1
    classDef netStyle fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef railStyle fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#616161,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    class na1,nb1,na2,nb2 netStyle
    class top1,top2,bot1,bot2,parTop,parBot railStyle
```

## 4.4 Interconnection Type → Parameter Mapping Flowchart

The following flowchart helps in selecting the correct parameter type based on the connection topology.

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([Two Port Networks to Connect]) --> q1{Connection Type?}
    q1 -->|Series| seriesLogic[Use Z parameters]
    q1 -->|Parallel| parallelLogic[Use Y parameters]
    q1 -->|Cascade| cascadeLogic[Use T parameters]
    q1 -->|Series Parallel| spLogic[Use h parameters]
    q1 -->|Parallel Series| psLogic[Use g parameters]
    seriesLogic --> seriesOp[Z total = Z A + Z B]
    parallelLogic --> parallelOp[Y total = Y A + Y B]
    cascadeLogic --> cascadeOp[T total = T A times T B]
    spLogic --> spOp[h total = h A + h B]
    psLogic --> psOp[g total = g A + g B]
    seriesOp --> result[Overall Network Parameters]
    parallelOp --> result
    cascadeOp --> result
    spOp --> result
    psOp --> result
    classDef decisionStyle fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF8F00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef processStyle fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00796B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef resultStyle fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    class q1 decisionStyle
    class seriesLogic,parallelLogic,cascadeLogic,spLogic,psLogic,seriesOp,parallelOp,cascadeOp,spOp,psOp processStyle
    class start,result resultStyle
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision-Making Heuristic for KTU Exams:**
> - **Currents shared?** → Z-parameters → Series
> - **Voltages shared?** → Y-parameters → Parallel
> - **Output feeds next input?** → T-parameters → Cascade

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Conceptual Short-Answer
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
> When two two-port networks are connected in series, why are Z-parameters used to describe the resulting network?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a series connection, the **same current** flows through both port 1 terminals of the two networks, and similarly the same current flows through both port 2 terminals. By Kirchhoff's Voltage Law, the **port voltages add algebraically**.

Z-parameters express port voltages as linear functions of port currents ($V = Z \cdot I$). Since the currents are identical for both networks and the voltages add, the Z-parameter matrices add element-wise:

$$[Z_{total}] = [Z_a] + [Z_b]$$

This direct additive property is what makes Z-parameters the natural choice for series-connected two-port networks. (3 marks)

---

### Question A2: Definitional Short-Answer
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> State the conditions under which two two-port networks can be validly connected in parallel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

For a valid parallel connection of two two-port networks N_a and N_b, the following conditions must be satisfied:

1. The voltage across port 1 of N_a must equal the voltage across port 1 of N_b: $V_1^a = V_1^b$ (1 mark)

2. The voltage across port 2 of N_a must equal the voltage across port 2 of N_b: $V_2^a = V_2^b$ (1 mark)

3. The total port currents are the algebraic sum of the individual network currents (KCL): $I_1 = I_1^a + I_1^b$ and $I_2 = I_2^a + I_2^b$ (1 mark)

Under these conditions, the overall Y-parameters are given by $[Y] = [Y_a] + [Y_b]$.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

### Question Choice A: Series Connection Analysis (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
> Two two-port networks are connected in series. The Z-parameters of the individual networks are:
>
> $$[Z_a] = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 4 \\ 4 & 12 \end{bmatrix} \, \Omega \quad \text{and} \quad [Z_b] = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 2 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \, \Omega$$
>
> **(a)** Determine the Z-parameters of the overall series-connected network. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Verify whether the resulting network is reciprocal, and explain what this means physically. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Overall Z-Parameters [7 marks]:**

For series connection, the Z-parameter matrices add directly:

$$[Z_{total}] = [Z_a] + [Z_b]$$

**Step 1:** Set up the matrix addition:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 4 \\ 4 & 12 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 2 & 10 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute element-wise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{11} &= 8 + 6 = 14 \, \Omega \\
Z_{12} &= 4 + 2 = 6 \, \Omega \\
Z_{21} &= 4 + 2 = 6 \, \Omega \\
Z_{22} &= 12 + 10 = 22 \, \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Express the result:

$$
[Z_{total}] = \begin{bmatrix} 14 & 6 \\ 6 & 22 \end{bmatrix} \, \Omega
$$

**(7 marks — full marks for correctly performing element-wise matrix addition)**

**Part (b) — Reciprocity Verification [7 marks]:**

**Step 1:** Recall the reciprocity condition: A two-port network is **reciprocal** if and only if $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$. (2 marks)

**Step 2:** Check the off-diagonal elements of the combined network:

$$Z_{12} = 6 \, \Omega \quad \text{and} \quad Z_{21} = 6 \, \Omega$$

Since $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 6 \, \Omega$, the **reciprocity condition is satisfied**. (3 marks)

**Step 3:** Physical interpretation:

A reciprocal network is one in which the transfer impedance from port 1 to port 2 equals the transfer impedance from port 2 to port 1. Physically, this means: if a voltage source $V_j$ applied at port $j$ produces a current $I_i$ at port $i$ (with all other ports open-circuited), then applying that same voltage at port $i$ will produce the same current at port $j$. This is a manifestation of the **reciprocity theorem**, which holds for any linear, passive, bilateral network made of standard passive elements (R, L, C, transformers with appropriate winding ratios). (2 marks)

**Conclusion:** The series-connected network is **reciprocal**, confirming the theorem that reciprocal networks connected in series yield a reciprocal network.

---

### Question Choice B: Parallel Connection Analysis (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> Two two-port networks are connected in parallel. The Y-parameters of the individual networks are:
>
> $$[Y_a] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.1 \\ -0.2 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix} \, S \quad \text{and} \quad [Y_b] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & -0.1 \\ -0.1 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} \, S$$
>
> **(a)** Determine the Y-parameters of the overall parallel-connected network and verify its reciprocity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** If a voltage source of $V_1 = 10 \, V$ and $V_2 = 5 \, V$ is applied to the input ports, find the input currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ at the ports. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Overall Y-Parameters and Reciprocity [7 marks]:**

For parallel connection, the Y-parameter matrices add directly:

$$[Y_{total}] = [Y_a] + [Y_b]$$

**Step 1:** Set up the matrix addition:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} Y_{11} & Y_{12} \\ Y_{21} & Y_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & -0.1 \\ -0.2 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0.3 & -0.1 \\ -0.1 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute element-wise:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{11} &= 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8 \, S \\
Y_{12} &= -0.1 + (-0.1) = -0.2 \, S \\
Y_{21} &= -0.2 + (-0.1) = -0.3 \, S \\
Y_{22} &= 0.4 + 0.6 = 1.0 \, S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Express the result:

$$
[Y_{total}] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & -0.2 \\ -0.3 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix} \, S
$$

**Step 4:** Check reciprocity. For a Y-parameter network, reciprocity requires $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$. (1 mark)

Since $Y_{12} = -0.2 \, S$ and $Y_{21} = -0.3 \, S$, we have $Y_{12} \neq Y_{21}$. **The combined network is NOT reciprocal.** (2 marks)

**Physical reason:** Although network B is reciprocal (Y₁₂ᵇ = Y₂₁ᵇ = -0.1), network A is **non-reciprocal** (Y₁₂ᵃ = -0.1 but Y₂₁ᵃ = -0.2). When a non-reciprocal network is combined with any other network, the result is non-reciprocal. This demonstrates that reciprocity is **not preserved** when non-reciprocal networks are interconnected in parallel. (1 mark)

**(7 marks — 5 for computation, 2 for reciprocity check)**

**Part (b) — Finding Port Currents [7 marks]:**

**Step 1:** Use the Y-parameter equations of the combined network:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= Y_{11} \cdot V_1 + Y_{12} \cdot V_2 \\
I_2 &= Y_{21} \cdot V_1 + Y_{22} \cdot V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given voltages $V_1 = 10 \, V$ and $V_2 = 5 \, V$ and the computed Y-parameters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= (0.8)(10) + (-0.2)(5) \\
I_1 &= 8.0 + (-1.0) \\
I_1 &= 7.0 \, A
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2 &= (-0.3)(10) + (1.0)(5) \\
I_2 &= -3.0 + 5.0 \\
I_2 &= 2.0 \, A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Express the final answer with units:

$$I_1 = 7.0 \, A, \quad I_2 = 2.0 \, A$$

**(7 marks — 2 for writing equations, 3 for correct substitution, 2 for final answers with proper units)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Mismatched parameter usage (2-3 marks lost)**: Students often incorrectly add Z-parameters for parallel connection or Y-parameters for series connection. **Always check which physical quantity is shared** (current → Z; voltage → Y).
>
> 2. **Forgetting to verify reciprocity (1-2 marks lost)**: KTU examiners explicitly look for the reciprocity check ($Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ or $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$) as a separate sub-step. Always include it explicitly.
>
> 3. **Unit errors (1 mark lost)**: Z-parameters are in **Ohms (Ω)**; Y-parameters are in **Siemens (S)**. Mixing them up is a common mistake.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in off-diagonal elements (1-2 marks lost)**: Pay careful attention to negative signs when adding elements like $Y_{12} = -0.1 + (-0.1) = -0.2$, not $+0.2$.
>
> 5. **Not stating validity conditions (1 mark lost)**: For full marks, mention the conditions for valid series/parallel connection (current equality for series, voltage equality for parallel).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- ☐ **Series connection** → use **Z-parameters**; the matrices add element-wise: $[Z] = [Z_a] + [Z_b]$.
- ☐ **Parallel connection** → use **Y-parameters**; the matrices add element-wise: $[Y] = [Y_a] + [Y_b]$.
- ☐ **Cascade connection** → use **T-parameters (ABCD)**; the matrices multiply: $[T] = [T_a] \cdot [T_b]$ (order matters in multiplication).
- ☐ **Series-Parallel** (input in series, output in parallel) → use **h-parameters**.
- ☐ **Parallel-Series** (input in parallel, output in series) → use **g-parameters**.
- ☐ **Validity conditions**:
  - **Series**: Same current flows through both ports ($I_1^a = I_1^b$ and $I_2^a = I_2^b$).
  - **Parallel**: Same voltage across both ports ($V_1^a = V_1^b$ and $V_2^a = V_2^b$).
- ☐ **Reciprocity check**:
  - In Z-form: $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$
  - In Y-form: $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$
  - In T-form: $AD - BC = 1$ (determinant condition)
  - In h-form: $h_{12} = -h_{21}$
- ☐ **Symmetry check**:
  - In Z-form: $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$
  - In Y-form: $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$
- ☐ **Units to remember**: Z in **Ω**, Y in **S**, T and h parameters are **dimensionless ratios**.
- ☐ **Matrix addition is commutative**: $[Z_a] + [Z_b] = [Z_b] + [Z_a]$ — order does not matter for series/parallel.
- ☐ **Matrix multiplication is NOT commutative** for cascade: $[T_a] \cdot [T_b] \neq [T_b] \cdot [T_a]$ — order DOES matter.
- ☐ **Key physical insight**: Z-parameters are "natural" for series because they relate voltage (summed) to current (shared). Y-parameters are "natural" for parallel because they relate current (summed) to voltage (shared).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocal and Symmetrical Two Port Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT414 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reciprocal and Symmetrical Two Port Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Two-Port Network** is an electrical network with two distinct pairs of terminals — one designated as the **input port** (Port 1, with current $I_1$ entering and voltage $V_1$ across it) and the other as the **output port** (Port 2, with current $I_2$ leaving and voltage $V_2$ across it). The behaviour of such a network is completely characterized by a set of **two-port parameters** that linearly relate the port voltages and currents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Key Special Properties of Two-Port Networks (KTU Module 4 Focus)**
>
> **1. Reciprocal (Bilateral) Two-Port Network:**
> A two-port network is said to be **reciprocal** if the ratio of excitation to response remains the same when the positions of the excitation and response are interchanged. Mathematically, the **Transmission Parameters (ABCD)** satisfy the condition:
> $$AD - BC = 1$$
>
> **2. Symmetrical Two-Port Network:**
> A two-port network is said to be **symmetric** if its input and output ports can be interchanged without any change in the port voltages and currents. Mathematically, in addition to the reciprocity condition, the **Transmission Parameters (ABCD)** satisfy:
> $$A = D$$
>
> A **symmetric network is always reciprocal**, but a reciprocal network is NOT necessarily symmetric.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Talking Through a Wall" Example**
>
> Imagine two rooms (Port 1 and Port 2) separated by a thick wall containing a network of pipes, wires, and resistors. You can shout into Room 1 (apply an excitation) and measure the sound reaching Room 2 (response).
>
> - **Reciprocity** is like saying: *"If I shout in Room 1 and you measure the sound in Room 2, you get the SAME loudness as if you shouted in Room 2 and measured the sound in Room 1."* The wall (network) treats both directions of energy transfer identically. This is true if the wall is made of **passive, linear elements** (resistors, capacitors, inductors, transformers) — but **NOT** if it contains an amplifier, transistor, or any active unilateral element.
>
> - **Symmetry** goes one step further: *"Not only is the transfer the same in both directions, but the wall (network) is also physically identical when viewed from either room."* The input impedance you measure looking into Room 1 (with Room 2 open) is the same as the input impedance you measure looking into Room 2 (with Room 1 open).
>
> Think of a **T-network with equal series arms and a shunt middle arm** — that is a classic symmetric reciprocal network. Now make the two series arms unequal — it is still reciprocal, but no longer symmetric.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Symbols in KTU Two-Port Theory**
>
> - **Z-parameters (Open-Circuit Impedance):** Units in **Ohms ($\Omega$)** — measured by leaving one port **open-circuited**.
> - **Y-parameters (Short-Circuit Admittance):** Units in **Siemens (S)** — measured by **short-circuiting** one port.
> - **h-parameters (Hybrid):** Mixed units — $h_{11}$ in $\Omega$, $h_{22}$ in $S$, $h_{12}, h_{21}$ dimensionless.
> - **ABCD-parameters (Transmission):** Dimensionless — used for **cascaded** networks.
> - **g-parameters (Inverse Hybrid):** Mixed units — inverse of h-parameters.

---

## 1.4 Why Study Reciprocal and Symmetric Networks?

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance**
> - **Filter Design:** Symmetric lattice and bridge networks form the backbone of classical filter theory.
> - **Transmission Lines:** Every uniform transmission line is a symmetric, reciprocal two-port.
> - **Power Systems:** Transformers are reciprocal and symmetric (for ideal ones) — this simplifies short-circuit analysis.
> - **Antenna Theory:** The **Reciprocity Theorem** (used in antenna design) directly follows from the property of reciprocal two-port networks.
> - **Network Simplification:** Recognizing reciprocity and symmetry can halve the number of independent parameters, drastically reducing computation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Six Two-Port Parameter Sets

A two-port network can be described by **six different sets of parameters**, depending on which two of the four variables ($V_1, V_2, I_1, I_2$) are chosen as independent. The KTU Module 4 syllabus emphasizes the conditions for reciprocity and symmetry in all six sets.

### 2.1.1 The Six Parameter Definitions (Quick Recap)

**Z-Parameters (Open-Circuit Impedance Parameters):**

$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$$
$$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$$

**Y-Parameters (Short-Circuit Admittance Parameters):**

$$I_1 = Y_{11} V_1 + Y_{12} V_2$$
$$I_2 = Y_{21} V_1 + Y_{22} V_2$$

**h-Parameters (Hybrid Parameters):**

$$V_1 = h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2$$
$$I_2 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2$$

**g-Parameters (Inverse Hybrid Parameters):**

$$I_1 = g_{11} V_1 + g_{12} I_2$$
$$V_2 = g_{21} V_1 + g_{22} I_2$$

**ABCD-Parameters (Transmission / General Circuit Parameters):**

$$V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$$
$$I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$$

**A'B'C'D'-Parameters (Inverse Transmission Parameters):**

$$V_2 = A' V_1 - B' I_1$$
$$I_2 = C' V_1 - D' I_1$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** in the ABCD and A'B'C'D' equations arises because the current $I_2$ is conventionally defined as **leaving** Port 2 (not entering). KTU examiners often test this sign convention — be careful.

---

## 2.2 Conditions for Reciprocity and Symmetry — Parameter-Wise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reciprocity and symmetry conditions are **most simply stated** using the ABCD parameters, but they can be transformed into equivalent conditions on any other parameter set.

### 2.2.1 ABCD Parameters (Easiest Form)

- **Reciprocal:** $AD - BC = 1$
- **Symmetric:** $A = D$ (and hence, by reciprocity, $A^2 - BC = 1$)

### 2.2.2 Z-Parameters

- **Reciprocal:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$
- **Symmetric:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ **AND** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$

### 2.2.3 Y-Parameters

- **Reciprocal:** $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$
- **Symmetric:** $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ **AND** $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$

### 2.2.4 h-Parameters

- **Reciprocal:** $h_{12} = -h_{21}$
- **Symmetric:** $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ **AND** $\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = 1$

### 2.2.5 g-Parameters

- **Reciprocal:** $g_{12} = -g_{21}$
- **Symmetric:** $g_{12} = -g_{21}$ **AND** $\Delta g = g_{11} g_{22} - g_{12} g_{21} = 1$

### 2.2.6 A'B'C'D' Parameters

- **Reciprocal:** $A' D' - B' C' = 1$
- **Symmetric:** $A' = D'$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER REFERENCE TABLE — Reciprocity and Symmetry Conditions**

| Parameter Set | Independent Variables | **Reciprocity Condition** | **Symmetry Condition (additional)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Z** | $I_1, I_2$ | $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ | $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ (also) |
| **Y** | $V_1, V_2$ | $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ | $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ (also) |
| **h** | $I_1, V_2$ | $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ | $\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = 1$ |
| **g** | $V_1, I_2$ | $g_{12} = -g_{21}$ | $\Delta g = g_{11} g_{22} - g_{12} g_{21} = 1$ |
| **ABCD** | $V_2, -I_2$ | $AD - BC = 1$ | $A = D$ |
| **A'B'C'D'** | $V_1, -I_1$ | $A' D' - B' C' = 1$ | $A' = D'$ |

**Conversion Identity (useful for derivations):**

$$A' = \frac{A}{\Delta T}, \quad B' = \frac{B}{\Delta T}, \quad C' = \frac{C}{\Delta T}, \quad D' = \frac{D}{\Delta T}, \quad \text{where } \Delta T = AD - BC$$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Where These Conditions Matter**
> - **Passive RLC networks** are **always reciprocal** (no active source, no unilateral element).
> - **Transistor amplifiers in h-parameter model** are typically **non-reciprocal** ($h_{12} \ne -h_{21}$ in general).
> - **Gyrators** are **non-reciprocal** — they are used in active filter design precisely to break reciprocity.
> - **Cascaded filter sections** are analyzed using ABCD parameters, so the **$AD - BC = 1$** test is the most practical one in filter design.
> - **Two-port networks containing controlled sources** (dependent sources) can be **non-reciprocal**, which is a frequent KTU problem theme.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reciprocity Condition in ABCD Parameters ($AD - BC = 1$)

### Starting Point — Definition of ABCD Parameters

By definition, the transmission parameters are defined as:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= A V_2 - B I_2 \\
I_1 &= C V_2 - D I_2
\end{aligned}$$

where the constants $A, B, C, D$ are dimensionless (and have units only in Z-parameters set).

### Step 1: Express Each Parameter Physically

**Parameter A** is found by setting $I_2 = 0$ (output port open-circuited):
$$A = \left. \frac{V_1}{V_2} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$$
This is the **reverse open-circuit voltage ratio**.

**Parameter B** is found by setting $V_2 = 0$ (output port short-circuited):
$$B = \left. -\frac{V_1}{I_2} \right|_{V_2 = 0}$$
This is the **negative reverse short-circuit transfer impedance**.

**Parameter C** is found by setting $I_2 = 0$:
$$C = \left. \frac{I_1}{V_2} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$$
This is the **reverse open-circuit transfer admittance**.

**Parameter D** is found by setting $V_2 = 0$:
$$D = \left. -\frac{I_1}{I_2} \right|_{V_2 = 0}$$
This is the **negative reverse short-circuit current ratio**.

### Step 2: Apply the Reciprocity Theorem

The **Reciprocity Theorem** states that in any linear, bilateral, passive network, the ratio of excitation to response is interchanged. For a two-port, this means:

$$\begin{aligned}
\left. \frac{V_2}{I_1} \right|_{I_2 = 0} &= \left. \frac{V_1}{I_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0}
\end{aligned}$$

In other words, the **open-circuit voltage at Port 2 due to a current $I_1$ injected at Port 1** equals the **open-circuit voltage at Port 1 due to a current $I_2$ injected at Port 2** (with the other port open).

### Step 3: Convert to ABCD Form

- Left side: $\left. \dfrac{V_2}{I_1} \right|_{I_2 = 0}$. From $I_1 = C V_2$ (when $I_2 = 0$), we get $\dfrac{V_2}{I_1} = \dfrac{1}{C}$.

- Right side: $\left. \dfrac{V_1}{I_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0}$. From $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2 = 0 \Rightarrow V_2 = \dfrac{D I_2}{C}$. Substituting into $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= A \cdot \frac{D I_2}{C} - B I_2 \\
V_1 &= I_2 \left( \frac{AD}{C} - B \right) \\
V_1 &= I_2 \left( \frac{AD - BC}{C} \right) \\
\frac{V_1}{I_2} &= \frac{AD - BC}{C}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Equate and Simplify

By reciprocity, $\dfrac{1}{C} = \dfrac{AD - BC}{C}$. Multiplying both sides by $C$ (assuming $C \ne 0$):

$$\boxed{1 = AD - BC}$$

This is the **reciprocity condition in ABCD parameters**, a KTU board-exam favourite.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Symmetry Condition in ABCD Parameters ($A = D$)

### Starting Point

A network is **symmetric** if exchanging Port 1 and Port 2 leaves the input-output relationship unchanged. For ABCD parameters, this means that the **reverse transmission parameters ($A', B', C', D'$)** must equal the **forward transmission parameters ($A, B, C, D$)**.

### Step 1: Write the Reverse Transmission Equations

By definition, the reverse transmission (A'B'C'D') relates the output to the input:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_2 &= A' V_1 - B' I_1 \\
I_2 &= C' V_1 - D' I_1
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Symmetry Implies $A' = A, B' = B, C' = C, D' = D$

This is the **formal definition of network symmetry**.

### Step 3: Use the Conversion Formulas

From the relationship between ABCD and A'B'C'D' (derived by inverting the ABCD matrix):

$$A' = \frac{A}{AD - BC}, \quad B' = \frac{B}{AD - BC}, \quad C' = \frac{C}{AD - BC}, \quad D' = \frac{D}{AD - BC}$$

For a reciprocal network, $AD - BC = 1$, so these simplify to:

$$A' = A, \quad B' = B, \quad C' = C, \quad D' = D \quad \text{(automatically true!)}$$

This shows that **every reciprocal network has $A' = A$ etc.**, but symmetry demands MORE: it demands the network to also be "input-output interchangeable in terms of structure."

### Step 4: Derive the Symmetry Condition

For symmetry, the input impedance at Port 1 (with Port 2 terminated in $Z_L$) must equal the input impedance at Port 2 (with Port 1 terminated in $Z_L$). The input impedance looking into Port 1 is:

$$Z_{in,1} = \frac{A Z_L + B}{C Z_L + D}$$

For symmetry: $Z_{in,1} = Z_{in,2}$. Now, $Z_{in,2}$ (looking into Port 2 of the reverse network) is:

$$Z_{in,2} = \frac{D Z_L + B}{C Z_L + A}$$

For $Z_{in,1} = Z_{in,2}$ for **all** $Z_L$, we need:

$$\frac{A Z_L + B}{C Z_L + D} = \frac{D Z_L + B}{C Z_L + A}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$\begin{aligned}
(A Z_L + B)(C Z_L + A) &= (D Z_L + B)(C Z_L + D) \\
A C Z_L^2 + A^2 Z_L + B C Z_L + A B &= D C Z_L^2 + D^2 Z_L + B C Z_L + B D
\end{aligned}$$

Simplifying (cancelling $BC Z_L$ from both sides):

$$AC Z_L^2 + A^2 Z_L + AB = DC Z_L^2 + D^2 Z_L + BD$$

For this to hold **for all** $Z_L$, we equate coefficients of $Z_L^2$, $Z_L^1$, and $Z_L^0$:

- Coefficient of $Z_L^2$: $AC = DC \Rightarrow A = D$ (since $C \ne 0$)
- Coefficient of $Z_L^1$: $A^2 = D^2$ — automatically true if $A = D$
- Constant term: $AB = BD$ — automatically true if $A = D$

### Step 5: Conclusion

$$\boxed{A = D}$$

Combined with reciprocity ($AD - BC = 1$), this gives $A^2 - BC = 1$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reciprocity Condition in h-Parameters ($h_{12} = -h_{21}$)

### Starting Point — h-Parameter Definition

$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2 \\
I_2 &= h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 1: Express the Reciprocity Theorem in h-Form

Reciprocity requires:

$$\left. \frac{V_2}{I_1} \right|_{I_2 = 0} = \left. \frac{V_1}{I_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Left Side

Setting $I_2 = 0$:
$$0 = h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2 \Rightarrow V_2 = -\frac{h_{21}}{h_{22}} I_1$$
$$\left. \frac{V_2}{I_1} \right|_{I_2 = 0} = -\frac{h_{21}}{h_{22}}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Right Side

Setting $I_1 = 0$:
$$V_1 = h_{12} V_2$$
Also, from the second equation: $I_2 = h_{22} V_2 \Rightarrow V_2 = \dfrac{I_2}{h_{22}}$
Therefore: $V_1 = h_{12} \cdot \dfrac{I_2}{h_{22}}$
$$\left. \frac{V_1}{I_2} \right|_{I_1 = 0} = \frac{h_{12}}{h_{22}}$$

### Step 4: Equate and Conclude

$$-\frac{h_{21}}{h_{22}} = \frac{h_{12}}{h_{22}}$$

Multiplying both sides by $h_{22}$:

$$\boxed{h_{12} = -h_{21}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Symmetry Condition in h-Parameters ($\Delta h = 1$)

For a network to be symmetric (input impedance same in both directions), we additionally need $h_{11} = 1/h_{22}$ (this is equivalent to $\Delta h = 1$).

**Verification:** The input impedance at Port 1 (with $Z_L$ at Port 2) is:
$$Z_{in,1} = h_{11} - \frac{h_{12} h_{21}}{h_{22} + Y_L}$$

The input impedance at Port 2 (looking into the reverse, with $Z_L$ at Port 1) is, by the conversion formulas (g-parameters are the inverse hybrid):
$$Z_{in,2} = \frac{1}{g_{22} - g_{12} g_{21} / (g_{11} + Z_L^{-1})}$$

For these to be equal for all $Z_L$, we require $\Delta h = 1$ (and reciprocity, $h_{12} = -h_{21}$).

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Proving Reciprocity and Symmetry of a T-Network

**Problem:** Consider a symmetric T-network with series arm impedance $Z_a$ on each side and shunt arm impedance $Z_b$ in the middle. Show that it is reciprocal and symmetric using Z-parameters.

### Step 1: Write the T-Network Equations

By KCL/KVL on the T-network:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= Z_a I_1 + Z_b (I_1 + I_2) = (Z_a + Z_b) I_1 + Z_b I_2 \\
V_2 &= Z_b (I_1 + I_2) + Z_a I_2 = Z_b I_1 + (Z_a + Z_b) I_2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2: Read Off the Z-Parameters

$$Z_{11} = Z_a + Z_b, \quad Z_{12} = Z_b, \quad Z_{21} = Z_b, \quad Z_{22} = Z_a + Z_b$$

### Step 3: Check Reciprocity

$$Z_{12} = Z_b = Z_{21} \quad \checkmark$$

**Network is reciprocal.**

### Step 4: Check Symmetry

$$Z_{11} = Z_a + Z_b = Z_{22} \quad \checkmark$$

**Network is also symmetric.**

### Step 5: Verify via ABCD Parameters

Converting Z to ABCD:

$$A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{Z_a + Z_b}{Z_b}, \quad B = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}} = \frac{Z_a^2 + 2 Z_a Z_b}{Z_b}, \quad C = \frac{1}{Z_{21}} = \frac{1}{Z_b}, \quad D = \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{Z_a + Z_b}{Z_b}$$

Check $AD - BC$:
$$AD - BC = \left(\frac{Z_a + Z_b}{Z_b}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{Z_a^2 + 2Z_a Z_b}{Z_b}\right)\left(\frac{1}{Z_b}\right) = \frac{Z_a^2 + 2Z_a Z_b + Z_b^2 - Z_a^2 - 2Z_a Z_b}{Z_b^2} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

Check $A = D$: $A = \dfrac{Z_a + Z_b}{Z_b} = D$ $\checkmark$

**Network is reciprocal and symmetric, as expected.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Port Network — General Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical two-port network structure with all four port variables, parameter sets, and reciprocity/symmetry decision points.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1A(["+ V1 −"]):::port --> N1["Linear\nPassive / Active\nTwo-Port Network\nN"]:::core
    N1 --> P2A(["+ V2 −"]):::port
    P1B(["I1 →"]):::port --> N1
    N1 --> P2B(["← I2"]):::port
    N1:::core
    classDef port fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#6d4c00,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Testing Reciprocity and Symmetry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Start: Compute all\nsix two-port\nparameter sets"]):::startNode
    checkABCD{"AD − BC = 1?"}:::decision
    checkAD{"A = D?"}:::decision
    recOnly["Network is\nRECIPROCAL only\n(not symmetric)"]:::result
    recSym["Network is\nRECIPROCAL and\nSYMMETRIC"]:::result
    notRec["Network is\nNEITHER reciprocal\nNOR symmetric"]:::result
    start --> checkABCD
    checkABCD -- "Yes" --> checkAD
    checkABCD -- "No" --> notRec
    checkAD -- "Yes" --> recSym
    checkAD -- "No" --> recOnly
    classDef startNode fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Six Parameter Sets and Their Reciprocity/Symmetry Tests

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    P1 --> Z["Z-parameters\nZ11, Z12, Z21, Z22\nImpedance, Ω"]:::param

    P1 --> H["h-parameters\nh11, h12, h21, h22\nHybrid, mixed"]:::param

    P1 --> TI["A'B'C'D'-parameters\nReverse transmission"]:::param
    Z --> ZT["REC: Z12 = Z21\nSYM: Z11 = Z22"]:::test
    Y --> YT["REC: Y12 = Y21\nSYM: Y11 = Y22"]:::test
    H --> HT["REC: h12 = −h21\nSYM: Δh = 1"]:::test
    G --> GT["REC: g12 = −g21\nSYM: Δg = 1"]:::test
    T --> TT["REC: AD − BC = 1\nSYM: A = D"]:::test
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State and explain the conditions for a two-port network to be (i) reciprocal and (ii) symmetric. Express the conditions in terms of ABCD parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

A two-port network can be classified as **reciprocal** and/or **symmetric** based on specific parameter relationships.

**(i) Reciprocal Network:** A two-port network is said to be reciprocal if the **transmission parameters satisfy**:
$$AD - BC = 1$$
This means the network is bilateral — the response at one port due to excitation at the other port is the same regardless of the direction of excitation.

**(ii) Symmetric Network:** A two-port network is said to be symmetric if, in addition to being reciprocal, it satisfies:
$$A = D$$
This means the network looks identical electrically from either port — the input impedance is the same whether measured at Port 1 (with Port 2 terminated) or at Port 2 (with Port 1 terminated in the same load).

**Equivalent conditions in other parameters:**
- In Z-parameters: Reciprocal $\Rightarrow Z_{12} = Z_{21}$; Symmetric $\Rightarrow Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ (also).
- In Y-parameters: Reciprocal $\Rightarrow Y_{12} = Y_{21}$; Symmetric $\Rightarrow Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ (also).

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**A two-port network has the following Z-parameters: $Z_{11} = 10 \Omega$, $Z_{12} = 4 \Omega$, $Z_{21} = 4 \Omega$, $Z_{22} = 8 \Omega$. Check whether the network is reciprocal and symmetric.**

**Model Answer:**

**Check for Reciprocity:**
For a reciprocal network, the condition is $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$.

Given: $Z_{12} = 4 \Omega$ and $Z_{21} = 4 \Omega$.

Since $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 4 \Omega$, the network is **RECIPROCAL** ✓

**Check for Symmetry:**
For a symmetric network, we additionally need $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$.

Given: $Z_{11} = 10 \Omega$ and $Z_{22} = 8 \Omega$.

Since $Z_{11} \ne Z_{22}$ ($10 \ne 8$), the network is **NOT SYMMETRIC** ✗

**Conclusion:** The network is **only reciprocal, not symmetric**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]

**(a)** Derive the conditions for reciprocity and symmetry of a two-port network in terms of **ABCD transmission parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the two-port network shown below, find the ABCD parameters and hence determine whether the network is reciprocal and symmetric. **(7 marks)**

```
      +--- Z1 ---+--------- Z2 ---+
      |          |               |
   +--V1--+      Z3           +--V2--+
      |          |               |
      +----------+               +-----+
   (Port 1)                  (Port 2)
```
where $Z_1 = 2\,\Omega$, $Z_2 = 6\,\Omega$, $Z_3 = 4\,\Omega$ (this is an asymmetric T-network).

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The final conditions are:

- **Reciprocity:** $AD - BC = 1$
- **Symmetry:** $A = D$ (in addition to reciprocity)

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

This is a T-network with **unequal series arms** ($Z_1 \ne Z_2$). The ABCD parameters for a T-network with series arm $Z_1$ (Port 1 side), series arm $Z_2$ (Port 2 side), and shunt arm $Z_3$ are:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= 1 + \frac{Z_1}{Z_3} \\
B &= Z_1 + Z_2 + \frac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_3} \\
C &= \frac{1}{Z_3} \\
D &= 1 + \frac{Z_2}{Z_3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Substituting the given values** ($Z_1 = 2\,\Omega$, $Z_2 = 6\,\Omega$, $Z_3 = 4\,\Omega$):

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= 1 + \frac{2}{4} = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5 \\
B &= 2 + 6 + \frac{2 \times 6}{4} = 8 + 3 = 11\,\Omega \\
C &= \frac{1}{4} = 0.25\,\text{S} \\
D &= 1 + \frac{6}{4} = 1 + 1.5 = 2.5
\end{aligned}$$

**Check for Reciprocity:**

$$AD - BC = (1.5)(2.5) - (11)(0.25)$$
$$AD - BC = 3.75 - 2.75 = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Network is RECIPROCAL.**

**Check for Symmetry:**

$$A = 1.5, \quad D = 2.5$$
$$A \ne D \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Network is NOT symmetric}$$

**Conclusion:** The network is **reciprocal but not symmetric** (because the series arms are unequal).

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** Explain with suitable derivations the conditions for **reciprocity** and **symmetry** of a two-port network in terms of **h-parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-port network has the following h-parameters:
$$h_{11} = 1000\,\Omega, \quad h_{12} = 0.0025, \quad h_{21} = 50, \quad h_{22} = 50\,\mu\text{S}$$
Test whether the network is **reciprocal** and **symmetric**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

Refer to the derivations in **Section 3.3** and **Section 3.4** of these notes. The final conditions are:

- **Reciprocity:** $h_{12} = -h_{21}$
- **Symmetry (in addition to reciprocity):** $\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = 1$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Check for Reciprocity:**

The condition is $h_{12} = -h_{21}$.

Given: $h_{12} = 0.0025$ and $h_{21} = 50$.

$$-h_{21} = -50$$

$$h_{12} = 0.0025 \ne -50$$

**The network is NOT reciprocal.** ✗

This is a typical result for a **transistor in common-emitter configuration**, where $h_{12}$ is very small (reverse voltage feedback) and $h_{21}$ is large (forward current gain).

**Check for Symmetry (irrelevant here, but for completeness):**

Since the network is not even reciprocal, it cannot be symmetric. We can verify $\Delta h$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta h &= h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} \\
\Delta h &= (1000)(50 \times 10^{-6}) - (0.0025)(50) \\
\Delta h &= 0.05 - 0.125 \\
\Delta h &= -0.075
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\Delta h \ne 1$ and reciprocity fails, the network is **neither reciprocal nor symmetric**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention in ABCD Parameters:** Many students forget the **negative sign** in the ABCD definition ($V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$, $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$). This negative sign is crucial — getting it wrong flips the sign of $B$ and $D$ and ruins the reciprocity test.
>
> 2. **Confusing "Symmetric" with "Reciprocal":** A common error is to declare a network "symmetric" just because $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$. This only proves reciprocity. Symmetry requires the **additional** condition $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ (or $A = D$).
>
> 3. **Using the Wrong Condition for h/g-Parameters:** Students often write $h_{12} = h_{21}$ instead of $h_{12} = -h_{21}$. The negative sign is **mandatory** and comes from the reciprocity theorem's phase relationship.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Check $C \ne 0$:** The derivation of $AD - BC = 1$ requires $C \ne 0$. If $C = 0$, the network is degenerate (no shunt path).
>
> 5. **Computing the Wrong Determinant:** For ABCD parameters, the reciprocity condition is $AD - BC = 1$, NOT $AD - BC = 0$. KTU examiners frequently test this exact distinction.
>
> 6. **Units Confusion in Mixed Parameters:** h-parameters have **mixed units** — never add $h_{11}$ (in $\Omega$) to $h_{22}$ (in S). The product $h_{11} h_{22}$ is dimensionless only for symmetry, which is what $\Delta h = 1$ confirms.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Reciprocal and Symmetrical Two-Port Networks**
>
> ✅ A **reciprocal** network satisfies $AD - BC = 1$ in ABCD parameters. Physically, it is **bilateral** (no controlled sources, no gyrators).
>
> ✅ A **symmetric** network satisfies $A = D$ (in addition to $AD - BC = 1$). Physically, the input impedance is the same looking into either port.
>
> ✅ **Symmetric $\Rightarrow$ Reciprocal**, but **Reciprocal $\not\Rightarrow$ Symmetric**.
>
> ✅ Equivalent reciprocity conditions across all six parameter sets:
> - Z: $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$
> - Y: $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$
> - h: $h_{12} = -h_{21}$
> - g: $g_{12} = -g_{21}$
> - ABCD: $AD - BC = 1$
> - A'B'C'D': $A'D' - B'C' = 1$
>
> ✅ Equivalent symmetry conditions across all six parameter sets:
> - Z: $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ (and $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$)
> - Y: $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ (and $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$)
> - h: $\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = 1$ (and $h_{12} = -h_{21}$)
> - g: $\Delta g = 1$ (and $g_{12} = -g_{21}$)
> - ABCD: $A = D$ (and $AD - BC = 1$)
> - A'B'C'D': $A' = D'$
>
> ✅ **Passive RLC networks** are always reciprocal. They are symmetric only if the network topology is mirror-symmetric about its center.
>
> ✅ **Networks with controlled sources** can be non-reciprocal (e.g., transistor models, op-amp circuits with feedback).
>
> ✅ The **ABCD parameters are the easiest** for testing reciprocity/symmetry because the condition is a single scalar equation ($AD - BC = 1$), whereas h/g parameters involve sign-sensitive mixed-unit products.
>
> ✅ The **negative sign** in ABCD and A'B'C'D' definitions ($V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$) arises because $I_2$ is defined as **leaving** Port 2, not entering. This is a KTU favourite trick question.
>
> ✅ **Practical test for reciprocity:** Inject a current $I_1$ at Port 1 (with Port 2 open) and measure the open-circuit voltage at Port 2. Repeat with current injected at Port 2 and measured at Port 1. Equal readings $\Rightarrow$ reciprocal.
>
> ✅ **Practical test for symmetry:** Measure $Z_{in}$ looking into Port 1 (with Port 2 shorted or open). Repeat looking into Port 2. Equal readings $\Rightarrow$ symmetric.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristic Impedance, Image Impedance and Propagation Constant (Derivation Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT414 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristic Impedance, Image Impedance and Propagation Constant (Derivation Not Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Characteristic Impedance (Z₀)?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Characteristic Impedance** of a *symmetrical* two-port (T or π) network is defined as the impedance measured at one pair of terminals when the other pair is terminated in an iteratively equal impedance such that the input impedance equals the terminating impedance.

Mathematically, if the network is symmetrical, then:

$$Z_0 = Z_{it} = Z_{ic} = \sqrt{Z_{oc} \cdot Z_{sc}}$$

where $Z_{oc}$ and $Z_{sc}$ are the **open-circuit** and **short-circuit** driving-point impedances at either port (equal for a symmetrical network).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight — The "Invisible Wire" Analogy:**
> Imagine shouting into a long hallway. If the hallway walls are perfectly absorbing, the echo dies out. But if the impedance of the wall matches that of the air in the hallway, sound travels smoothly with no reflection. **Characteristic impedance is the "natural electrical signature" of a network** — when you terminate a line with this exact impedance, energy flows through without any reflection at the junction. In KTU parlance, it is the *iterative impedance* for symmetric two-port networks.

### Why "Iterative"?
The term **iterative** comes from the Latin *iterare* (to repeat). If you cascade infinitely many identical symmetric sections, the input impedance at every section is the same — it *iterates* without change.

---

## 1.2 What is Image Impedance (Z_i)?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Image Impedances** $Z_{i1}$ and $Z_{i2}$ of an *asymmetrical* two-port network are a pair of impedances such that, when port 2 is terminated in $Z_{i2}$, the input impedance at port 1 is exactly $Z_{i1}$, and *vice versa*.

$$Z_{i1} = \sqrt{Z_{oc1} \cdot Z_{sc1}}$$

$$Z_{i2} = \sqrt{Z_{oc2} \cdot Z_{sc2}}$$

For a **symmetrical network**, $Z_{oc1} = Z_{oc2}$ and $Z_{sc1} = Z_{sc2}$, so $Z_{i1} = Z_{i2} = Z_0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Two-Way Mirror:**
> Think of image impedance as a *pair of glasses* that makes a network "see" itself at the other end. When port 1 looks into the network, it sees its own image $Z_{i1}$, and port 2 sees its own image $Z_{i2}$. This is the basis of **image-impedance matching**, the most fundamental concept for connecting filters, attenuators, and transmission-line sections in cascade.

---

## 1.3 What is Propagation Constant (γ)?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> The **Propagation Constant** $\gamma$ of a symmetrical two-port network is defined (when terminated in its characteristic impedance $Z_0$) as:
> $$\gamma = \frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{V_1 I_1}{V_2 I_2}\right) = \alpha + j\beta$$
> where $\alpha$ is the **attenuation constant** (in **Nepers**) and $\beta$ is the **phase constant** (in **radians**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Insight — The "Distance Cost" Concept:**
> Every time a signal passes through a network section, it pays a "cost" — some amplitude is lost ($\alpha$) and some phase is rotated ($\beta$). The propagation constant is the *per-section* cost. Cascading **N** identical sections multiplies the cost by **N**: the total propagation is $N\gamma$. In high-frequency PCB traces, telephone cables, and RF filters, $\gamma$ is the master descriptor of how a signal degrades and rotates as it travels.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Concept Hierarchy — How These Three Quantities Relate

The three quantities form a **logical pyramid** of two-port analysis:

| Level | Quantity | Applicable To | Defined Using |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Most general) | Image Impedances $Z_{i1}, Z_{i2}$ | Asymmetric networks | $Z_{oc}, Z_{sc}$ per port |
| 2 (Special case) | Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$ | Symmetric networks only | $\sqrt{Z_{oc} Z_{sc}}$ (same for both ports) |
| 3 (Behavioural) | Propagation Constant $\gamma$ | Requires image-matched termination | $\frac{1}{2}\ln(V_1I_1/V_2I_2)$ |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Quantity

**Why Image Impedance?**
- A *general* two-port has two independent port behaviours. We need **two** numbers ($Z_{i1}, Z_{i2}$) to fully describe its impedance interface.
- When terminated in image impedances, the network becomes "self-consistent" — power transfer is well-defined.

**Why Characteristic Impedance?**
- A *symmetric* two-port is fully described by **one** impedance number plus one propagation number.
- The "iterative" property makes cascade analysis clean: input impedance = $Z_0$ at every junction.

**Why Propagation Constant?**
- In a cascaded chain of identical matched sections, voltages and currents follow:
$$V_n = V_0 e^{-n\gamma}, \quad I_n = I_0 e^{-n\gamma}$$
- So $\gamma$ directly tells us **signal magnitude and phase** at any point in the chain.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is your **exam lifesaver**. Memorize the structure of these equations — the *form* matters more than the exact algebra for the 3-mark questions.

| # | Network | Parameter | Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | T (sym, $Z_1$ total series, $Z_2$ shunt) | $Z_{0T}$ | $\frac{1}{2}\sqrt{Z_1 \,(Z_1 + 4Z_2)}$ |
| 2 | π (sym, $Z_1$ each shunt, $Z_2$ series) | $Z_{0\pi}$ | $\sqrt{\dfrac{Z_1 \, Z_2}{1 + Z_2/Z_1}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{Z_1^2 Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2/2}} \ \text{(after algebra)}$ |
| 3 | T-asymmetric (arms $Z_1, Z_2, Z_3$) | $Z_{i1}$ | $\sqrt{(Z_1+Z_2)\left(Z_1 + \dfrac{Z_2 Z_3}{Z_2+Z_3}\right)}$ |
| 4 | T-asymmetric | $Z_{i2}$ | $\sqrt{(Z_2+Z_3)\left(Z_3 + \dfrac{Z_1 Z_2}{Z_1+Z_2}\right)}$ |
| 5 | Symmetric T | $\cosh \gamma$ | $1 + \dfrac{Z_1}{2 Z_2}$ |
| 6 | Symmetric π | $\cosh \gamma$ | $1 + \dfrac{Z_2}{2 Z_1}$ |
| 7 | Lattice (arms $Z_a, Z_b$) | $Z_0 = Z_i$ | $\sqrt{Z_a \cdot Z_b}$ |
| 8 | Symmetric two-port (general) | $\gamma$ | $\ln\!\left(A + \sqrt{A^2 - 1}\right) = \cosh^{-1}(A)$ |
| 9 | From $V,I$ in matched condition | $\gamma$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_1 I_1}{V_2 I_2}\right) = \alpha + j\beta$ |
| 10 | Attenuation in dB | $\alpha_{dB}$ | $8.686 \, \alpha$ (where $\alpha$ in Nepers) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Notation Rule Used:** In all formulas above, the absolute value of an impedance $Z$ is written as $\vert Z \vert$ (not $|Z|$) to remain compatible with markdown tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of These Quantities |
|---|---|
| **Telecommunication Lines** | Characteristic impedance (typically 50 Ω, 75 Ω, 600 Ω) defines the cable's natural mode; mismatch causes echoes in voice circuits. |
| **RF / Microwave Filters** | Image-impedance matching of cascaded filter sections is the cornerstone of modern filter design. |
| **PCB Trace Design** | Controlled-impedance traces (50 Ω microstrip) are designed using the $Z_0$ concept to prevent signal reflections. |
| **Attenuator Pads** | Lattice and T-attenuators are designed to have $Z_0$ = source/load impedance, so insertion of the pad does not disturb the source. |
| **Cascaded Amplifier Stages** | Each stage is image-matched to its neighbour for maximum power transfer and minimum VSWR. |

## 2.5 Key Properties of $\alpha$ and $\beta$

- **$\alpha > 0$** in a *passive* network (signal attenuates).
- **$\alpha < 0$** only in *active* networks (signal grows — used in amplifiers).
- **$\beta$** is the phase shift per section; for a chain of $N$ sections, total phase = $N\beta$.
- In a **lossless** network ($\alpha = 0$), $Z_0$ is purely **resistive** and $\gamma = j\beta$ is purely imaginary.
